Hy-Vee’s first female president, Donna Tweeten, dies at 63 | who13.com

DES MOINES, Iowa — Hy-Vee announced that Donna Tweeten, the company’s first female president, died following a battle with cancer.

Hy-Vee announced Thursday that Tweeten, age 63, had died April 8th following a battle with stage 4 pancreatic cancer. [Tweeten retired in January 2025](https://who13.com/news/iowa-news/first-female-president-of-hy-vee-drake-alum-retires/) but remained with Hy-Vee as a consultant up until her death.

Tweeten brought extensive advertising experience to Hy-Vee, having worked for firms including Davis Harrison Dion-Chicago and Anderson Communications, and played a significant role in helping the company reach national recognition. In 2006, Tweeten formally joined Hy-Vee as assistant vice president of communications and later held several positions, notably as the company’s chief marketing officer and later president.

The company says her leadership was instrumental in Hy-Vee becoming the first retailer to sign contracts with basketball star Caitlin Clark. Tweeten also secured major partnerships with other influential athletes, including Patrick Mahomes, Travis Kelce and Shawn Johnson East.

Jeremy Gosch, Chairman and CEO of Hy-Vee, expressed profound sadness over her loss. “We are simply heartbroken by the sudden loss of Donna Tweeten. Not only was Donna a trailblazer across Hy-Vee and the retail industry, she was a true friend and mentor to many, myself included.”

“Donna leaves behind a lasting legacy and will forever be remembered for her bold ideas, creative passion and commitment to excellence. We miss her deeply and words cannot express the sorrow that is being felt across our Hy-Vee family due to the impact she had on all of us.”

Throughout her career, Tweeten received national recognition across both the advertising and retail industries. She was named a Champion of Change by ADWEEK, honored as a Leading Women Forward recipient by Ad Age, and received the Mass Market Retailers’ Lifetime Achievement Award, among other accolades.

Tweeten is survived by her husband Alan and their five children, Carlie, Nick, Ben, Max and Ella, as well as her grandchildren.

Information regarding Tweeten’s funeral and service details has not yet been finalized. Those details are expected to be announced soon.

I recently joined leaders in San Francisco for [Charter](https://www.linkedin.com/company/charterworks?trk=public_post-text)'s Leading with AI Summit 2026, and now they've published a playbook recapping the conversations. Some themes stood out through the lends of a people-led organization: 1. The “messy middle” is real and it is where leadership matters most. Leadership will emerge as a key unlock to leverage the potential of AI. Leaders must connect AI to business outcomes, i.e. quality, growth, speed and new capabilities. 2. AI transformation is 10% technology and 90% people. Change management, clear communication about how jobs will evolve, creating space to experiment and rewarding skill-building unlock sustainable impact. Let’s be more positive and more practical about jobs. There's too much rhetoric about everything going away. The better questions are: What will AI create? How will it elevate roles, unlock new skills and expand opportunity? We have a responsibility to shape that future intentionally by investing in development, staying transparent about change and keeping people at the center. At [Walmart](https://www.linkedin.com/company/walmart?trk=public_post-text) we talk about being people-led and tech-powered. That mindset feels more relevant than ever. Technology will continue to evolve, but our values, our culture and our commitment to opportunity will lead the way. When the Bot Can’t Close the Deal: Why Walmart and OpenAI are Revamping Their Agentic Shopping Partnership. Conversion Friction in Agentic Commerce. Pivot in Retail AI: From Instant Checkout to Integrated Assistants AI Wants to Buy Your Groceries, but Humans Still Want a Cart. The "One-Click" AI Future Just hit a Speed Bump The grand experiment of "Instant Checkout" inside ChatGPT is being dismantled. After a highly publicized launch in late 2025, Walmart and OpenAI are "shaking up" their deal because the results were, frankly, underwhelming. While consumers are happy to talk to AI about what to buy, they aren't actually buying through it. Data shows that "in-chat" purchases converted at roughly one-third the rate of traditional web traffic. It turns out that when it comes to spending money, humans still prefer the familiarity of a cart they can see and a checkout page they can trust over a text-based "trust me" from a bot. Key Takeaways: The Conversion Gap: Walmart's EVP of Product confirmed that ChatGPT checkout performed significantly worse than their own website. The "frictionless" dream of Instant Checkout turned out to be "unsatisfying" for users who were forced to buy items individually rather than building a consolidated basket. The "Sparky" Pivot: Instead of relying on OpenAI's native checkout tools, Walmart is now embedding its own proprietary chatbot, Sparky, directly into ChatGPT and Google Gemini. This allows users to sync their actual Walmart carts across platforms, bringing the "intelligence" of the LLM to the "reliability" of Walmart’s own transaction engine. ## About

My passion is using field experiments to explore economic questions. I view field experiments as representing a unique manner in which to obtain data because they force the researcher to understand everyday phenomena, many of which we stumble upon frequently. Merely grasping the interrelationships of factors in field settings is not enough, however, as the field experimenter must then seek to understand i) the mechanisms underlying those relationships, and ii) more distant phenomena that have the same underlying structure. Until these dual goals are achieved, one cannot reap the true rewards of field experimentation. For a bunch of publications in the area of field experiments and behavioral economics please see http://www.fieldexperiments.com/. Some studies of interest: Why women are paid less than men for Gig jobs: https://ideas.repec.org/p/feb/natura/00634.html Why do people discriminate: https://ideas.repec.org/p/feb/natura/00299.html Why do inner city schools continue to fail: https://ideas.repec.org/p/feb/framed/00719.html Why do people give to charity: https://ideas.repec.org/p/feb/natura/00472.html Why we should run field experiments: https://ideas.repec.org/p/feb/artefa/00089.html The Science of Using Science (for policymaking): https://ideas.repec.org/p/feb/artefa/00670.html

# Walmart's Commerce-Powered Advertising Platform Shift

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

Flywheel•10K followers

1w

Walmart isn’t building a retail media network. They’re building a commerce-powered advertising platform. That’s the shift most people are still underestimating. When [Kimeko McCoy](https://www.linkedin.com/in/kimeko-mccoy-2018?trk=public_post-text) and I spoke this week about Walmart + Vizio, the question wasn’t: "Is Vizio working?" It was... "How does Vizio contribute to total Walmart performance?" Because that’s what brands actually care about now. Not channels. Not tactics. Not isolated ROAS. But how the entire system works together to drive total business growth. Walmart is at the cusp of going from Retail Media Network to advertising platform, stitching together: 🔹 Retail data 🔹 Off-site media 🔹 In-store media 🔹 CTV inventory (Vizio) 🔹 Identity + clean room infrastructure That’s not just a network. That’s an operating system for growth. But here’s the tension, while the ambition is clear, Walmart is still behind Amazon in the most critical area: a connected measurement approach. In AMC today, I can see the long-term impact of a Thursday Night Football ad on sales, and how it makes search, display, and everything downstream more efficient. My hot take: brand building is the single best performance lever if you are doing it right. Walmart is getting there. I’m bullish on where Data Ventures is going and we have seen advancements with Scintilla. If Walmart accelerates its clean room and measurement capabilities, Vizio adoption will follow. The question shifts from "Is Walmart catching up to Amazon?” to "Are we watching the early stages of a new advertising model?" My bet? Retail media doesn’t stay “retail media” much longer. It becomes the foundation of how all advertising works.

Walmart’s reported disappointment with OpenAI’s shopping partnership is a useful reality check for everyone betting big on conversational commerce (or AI SEO). If ChatGPT Instant Checkout is only converting at 1/3rd the rate of purchases on Walmart’s own site, the lesson is that distribution alone does not win commerce and subsequently, enterprises are running out of patience with results. Just because a product shows up inside a chatbot does not mean a customer is ready to buy there. Conversion still belongs to trust, intent, and habit. This translates to every industry. That same lesson applies more broadly as enterprises move from AI pilots to agents in production. Getting an agent into the workflow is only the beginning. The harder work is determining where autonomy actually adds value, where human judgment still matters, and how the end-customer, employee, and partner experience must be redesigned so people are willing to rely on it and trust it when the stakes are real. A lot of companies are about to run into this gap. Production is not the finish line - it is where the real adoption test begins. Kudos to the brave CxOs that are going first here and have bet their careers on this!

The latest move by [OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text) for scaling back its "Agentic Commerce" and [Walmart](https://www.linkedin.com/company/walmart?trk=public_post-text)'s dissatisfaction for its rollout is a great testament for why we never align ourselves with the ever-changing AI "marketing" terms that get thrown around throughout the AI hype cycle (remember GenAI?). You’d ask, why didn’t Verneek jump on the "Agentic Commerce" bandwagon, which, by many accounts is all we have stood for in the retail space for the past many years now? It’s because we knew better that what it takes to succeed in commerce, is way beyond a basic “chat” interface (even if you call it Agentic!), and we always avoid associating ourselves with the (ultimately failing) noise in the market. We rolled out our AI in the consumer retail industry with millions of shoppers way before it was “cool”, and I am so proud that we are consistently practicing all that we have preached for all the past many years. So, why did “Agentic Shopping” inside ChatGPT fail? We have helped deploy our AIs inside e-commerce and brick-and-mortar environments and it became abundantly clear to us that creating AI-Native shopping experiences are beyond a “chatbox”. To diagnose, let's set aside the terrible shopping experience of cramming an often-visual experience into a generic chat environment. The issue is not the checkout/payment transactions either. What is the core of the problem is getting the underlying “data” right! Every operationally-heavy retailer/merchant is a unique, messy universe of supply chain and inventory systems that don't talk to each other, bespoke customer loyalty and shopping history systems, nuanced markdown/discount logic that breaks if you apply it wrong, shipping and pickup rules that vary by status, postcode, weight, and day of the week, and whatever else is in your basket, and

We underestimate how much our internal dialogue shapes performance. You are in conversation with yourself all day long: before meetings, after presentations, during difficult decisions. For many high performers, that voice is more critical than constructive. [Erin McGoff](https://www.linkedin.com/in/erinmcgoff?trk=public_post-text) shares that confidence isn’t about eliminating self-doubt. It’s about reframing it. Instead of: “I bombed that.” Try: “I stumbled on one answer. I can improve that.” The shift sounds small. The long-term impact isn’t. Listen to the full episode now on [fastersmarter.io](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http%3A%2F%2Ffastersmarter%2Eio&urlhash=BYQy&trk=public_post-text).

4d

- [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fchuk-okpalugo_mastercard-just-acquired-bvnk-for-18b-to-activity-7439669864061067264-vCgi&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

Mastercard just acquired BVNK for $1.8B to make sure stablecoins never route around Mastercard The play: extend the layer where enterprises already make routing, compliance, and settlement decisions to cover on-chain rails. Stablecoins, tokenized deposits, fiat. One platform. [Mastercard](https://www.linkedin.com/company/mastercard?trk=public_post-text) expects most financial institutions and fintechs will eventually offer digital currency services. This is a balance-sheet bet on owning that control surface. Stablecoin tech is increasingly reproducible. What's scarce is regulated distribution. Global licenses, enterprise trust, merchant relationships, compliance credibility. [BVNK](https://www.linkedin.com/company/bvnk?trk=public_post-text) already processes $30B annually for companies like Worldpay and Deel across 130+ countries. Mastercard gives it distribution gravity that no standalone infra company can match. By absorbing these capabilities into its network, Mastercard ensures every bank, fintech, and enterprise CFO can access stablecoin rails through a partner they already trust. No reason to build around it. Congrats to the BVNK team!

The companies with the most data may win the AI race. Think about it. For years, companies like Meta and Google have collected enormous amounts of user data. -Search behavior. -Social interactions. -Content preferences. -Advertising insights. That information creates a massive training advantage when building artificial intelligence systems. In theory, those companies should have been the first leaders in large language models. Yet the market initially shifted toward companies like OpenAI and tools backed by Microsoft. But something interesting is happening. This year we are already seeing major acceleration from Google. New AI products, faster releases, and deeper integration across their ecosystem. From a technology perspective, Google still has one of the strongest research foundations in the industry. One observation though is strategic positioning. Many of the newest AI tools from Google appear heavily consumer focused. Meanwhile, Microsoft is aggressively targeting the enterprise market through platforms like Microsoft Copilot. Listen my E-Book Here: [https://zurl.co/Ujlco](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Fzurl%2Eco%2FUjlco&urlhash=vzfr&trk=public_post-text) That raises an important strategic question. Will Google push harder into business AI tools? Because the company already has powerful enterprise products inside its ecosystem. Great discussions and inspiring perspectives last week at the [Debiopharm](https://ch.linkedin.com/company/debiopharminternational?trk=public_post-text) Digital Day! [Juan Ramón Troncoso-Pastoriza](https://ch.linkedin.com/in/juan-ram%C3%B3n-troncoso-pastoriza-4508323a?trk=public_post-text) and myself had the pleasure to attend the event. The agenda brought together leaders across AI, pharma, and data science to explore how artificial intelligence is reshaping drug development, from discovery to clinical trials and biomarker development. A few insights that stood out to me: 🔹 AI is moving from experimentation to infrastructure. The conversation around the current and future landscape of AI highlighted how organizations are transitioning from isolated pilots to embedding AI as a core capability across R&D. 🔹 Clinical trials are entering a data-centric era. AI-driven approaches are improving patient stratification, optimizing trial design, and enabling better use of real-world and multimodal data to increase trial efficiency and success rates. For us at [Tune Insight](https://ch.linkedin.com/company/tuneinsight?trk=public_post-text), these discussions reinforce an important point: the future of AI in healthcare depends on secure and collaborative data access. Many of the most promising AI applications require combining sensitive datasets across institutions: something that must be done while preserving [#privacy](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fprivacy&trk=public_post-text) and [#trust](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Ftrust&trk=public_post-text). Excited to continue the conversation and explore how privacy-preserving analytics can help unlock the next generation of AI-powered healthcare innovation. What has been most striking since the acquisition is the consistency of conversations we are having with enterprise legal teams. Across industries and jurisdictions, the themes are the same: - Increasing volume of data and regulatory complexity - Valuable data trapped in contracts and processes - Growing expectation from the business to do more, faster, and with greater insight AI is now seen as part of the answer, but many teams are still figuring out how to deploy it in a way that actually delivers ROI. Working directly with our customers to build systems of intelligence that: - unlock the value in their existing legal data - reduce dependency on individual knowledge - drive measurable outcomes across their contracting and legal workflows This is a trillion-dollar market undergoing real change. We are excited to be working with clients at the forefront of that shift, where adoption is accelerating quickly. ⁉️ [Estée Lauder](https://www.linkedin.com/company/est%C3%A9e-lauder?trk=public_post-text)+ [Puig](https://es.linkedin.com/company/puig?trk=public_post-text): 𝗽𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲… 𝗼𝗿 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗻𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗲? On paper, it’s a $40B beauty powerhouse built to compete with [L'Oréal](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/lor%C3%A9al?trk=public_post-text). But the market is already telling a different story: ⬇️ [Estée Lauder](https://www.linkedin.com/company/est%C3%A9e-lauder?trk=public_post-text) stock dropped -8–9% on the news ⬆️ [Puig](https://es.linkedin.com/company/puig?trk=public_post-text) jumped +13–16% Why? Because 𝘀𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲 ≠ 𝘀𝘆𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗴𝘆. Yes, the logic is clear: ✅ Stronger position in fragrance (potential ~15% share) ✅ Complementary portfolios (Lauder = skincare, Puig = fragrance) But the risks are real: ↗️ Massive integration complexity across cultures + portfolios ↗️ $6B potential financing pressure ↗️ Distraction from Lauder’s ongoing turnaround And here’s the bigger signal: This isn’t just strategy — it’s defensive. The beauty giants are consolidating because organic growth is slowing and indie brands are winning share. 𝗠𝘆 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲: ✅ History isn’t exactly on their side. ✅ Even strong beauty deals take years to pay off — ↘️ [Coty](https://www.linkedin.com/company/coty?trk=public_post-text)’s [Procter & Gamble](https://www.linkedin.com/company/procter-and-gamble?trk=public_post-text) beauty acquisition wiped out billions in value before stabilizing. ↘️ [LVMH](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/lvmh?trk=public_post-text)’s [Tiffany & Co.](https://www.linkedin.com/company/tiffany-and-co?trk=public_post-text) integration required heavy brand repositioning and margin resets. Two essential pieces of reading by [Theo Francis](https://www.linkedin.com/in/theofrancis?trk=public_post-text) in the [The Wall Street Journal](https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-wall-street-journal?trk=public_post-text) on the groundbreaking [Where You Work Matters](https://www.linkedin.com/company/where-you-work-matters1?trk=public_post-text) List, which launched today. The Journal captures the profound importance of this data to workers and businesses alike, and in a second story, the paper shares some of the practices that led us to recognize 22 companies with Platinum awards across all three of our good-jobs models. Did you know that [Procter & Gamble](https://www.linkedin.com/company/procter-and-gamble?trk=public_post-text) hires most of its workers right out of college? Or that [General Motors](https://www.linkedin.com/company/general-motors?trk=public_post-text) exposes young workers to a range of experiences early on? At [Qualtrics](https://www.linkedin.com/company/qualtrics?trk=public_post-text), managers no longer need to approve internal transfers. The main story emphasizes that at a time when advancements in AI and automation are roiling the labor market, workers are better off at companies with track records of advancing their employees’ careers, rather than those treating workers as expendable. As I note in the story, “Having mobility strength is like having done the physical training and having the whole canteen of water as you hike up that mountain”. The List assesses nearly 55,000 jobs across 1,750 large U.S. employers. # Scaling GenAI with MCP: Standardized Tool Ecosystem

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

[](https://in.linkedin.com/in/bala-venkatesh-67964247?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Bala Venkatesh](https://in.linkedin.com/in/bala-venkatesh-67964247?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)

Standard Chartered Bank•14K followers

2d Edited

- [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fbala-venkatesh-67964247_until-mcp-adding-a-new-tool-to-an-llm-system-activity-7440408757593018368-86H0&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

Until MCP, adding a new tool to an LLM system meant writing custom function every time. It worked… but didn’t scale. Today I tried MCP (Model Context Protocol). Instead of building integrations again and again, it felt like tools just plug into a common layer. Small shift, but changes how you design GenAI systems. Less effort on integration. More focus on building actual intelligence. Key shift: Before MCP → Tool-specific integration After MCP → Standardized, plug-and-play tool ecosystem Why this matters: • Faster integration of new tools • Cleaner architecture for multi-agent systems • Better scalability for production GenAI applications

# Henkel Acquires Olaplex for $1.4B, Sheds Low-Margin Assets

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

 

 

[](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pvsbond?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Peter V.S. Bond](https://www.linkedin.com/in/pvsbond?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) 

 3d 

 

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fpvsbond_cpg-retailmedia-olaplex-activity-7443036429326258178-h2NB&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

𝗢𝗹𝗮𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘅 → 𝗛𝗲𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗹. 𝗔𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗱𝗮𝘆, 𝗮𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘂𝗺 𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗼 𝗽𝗶𝘃𝗼𝘁. 💇♀️🇩🇪 [Henkel](https://de.linkedin.com/company/henkel?trk=public_post-text) just agreed to acquire [OLAPLEX](https://www.linkedin.com/company/olaplex-inc?trk=public_post-text) for ~$1.4 billion — a 55% premium to Wednesday’s close, but a humbling reminder that the brand went public at a $13.6B valuation in 2021. Let that sink in. 𝟵𝟬%+ 𝗼𝗳 𝗶𝘁𝘀 𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗸 𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲, 𝗴𝗼𝗻𝗲. But here’s what actually matters for our industry: 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗮 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝘀𝗮𝗹𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆 — 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝘆 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆. Henkel is doing exactly what the best operators in CPG are doing right now: ✂️ 𝗦𝗵𝗲𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗹𝗼𝘄-𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻 𝘄𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 — they divested their North American retailer brands business in 2025 🏆 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘂𝗺 𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 — Schwarzkopf + Not Your Mother’s + Olaplex is a serious hair care stack 🌍 𝗟𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗴𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵 — pairing Olaplex’s DTC and North American retail strength with Henkel’s international distribution is a genuine growth unlock And Henkel isn’t alone. [Unilever](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/unilever?trk=public_post-text), [Reckitt](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/reckitt?trk=public_post-text), [Nestlé](https://ch.linkedin.com/company/nestle-s-a-?trk=public_post-text)— the entire conglomerate class is playing the same game: 𝗳𝗲𝘄𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗱𝘀, 𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻𝘀, 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗹 𝗺𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗮 𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲. IMO, the era of the sprawling CPG portfolio is over. The era of the 𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱, 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘂𝗺-𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗼 is very much here. For brands navigating this landscape — whether you’re being acquired, acquiring, or watching from the sidelines — the question is the same: 𝗗𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗸

Prevention or Treatment? Why Big pHARMa is in business today. Prevention- removing a carcinogen from the water supply or banning a toxic food additive costs a company money and generates $0 in medical revenue. Treatment- A single patient can represent $500,000+ in revenue over several years. Ahh, jackpot. In the case of Pfizer, following the decline of COVID-19 vaccine sales, Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla pivoted the entire company toward cancer. He recently described the company's $43 billion acquisition of Seagen as a "bold move to tackle critical areas of unmet need." Analysts note that Pfizer is moving away from primary care (keeping people healthy) and toward "specialty oncology." Why? Because oncology drugs have some of the highest profit margins in the world and are shielded from the "patent cliffs" that kill off cheaper medications. In the case of Merck, CEO Rob Davis on a recent call highlighted that the company has line of sight to $70 billion in commercial opportunity by the mid-2030s. He stated, "Today, our business is anchored by an important set of commercial products that address critical unmet needs... representing the majority of our $70 billion of non-risk-adjusted commercial opportunity." Translation: Merck is explicitly counting on a steady stream of sick people to double their revenue from their current blockbuster, Keytruda. For Gilead, the biggest mistake they ever made was to make a drug that cured Hep C. Their sales subsequently plummeted. They'll never make that mistake again. And it goes on. These and other companies view the rising cancer rate not as a crisis to be solved by prevention, but as a growth driver. They are there to deliver SHAREHOLDER VALUE, and that means they LOVE CANCER. [Callio Therapeutics](https://www.linkedin.com/company/calliotx?trk=public_post-text) Advances ADC Innovation: First Patient Dosed in Phase I Trial of CLIO-8221 The future of cancer treatment is evolving; and Callio Therapeutics is taking a bold step forward. The company has successfully dosed the first patient in its Phase I clinical trial of CLIO-8221, a first-in-class dual-payload antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting HER2-expressing solid tumors. What makes CLIO-8221 different? Combines two powerful mechanisms: Topo1 inhibitor + ATR inhibitor Designed to overcome resistance seen in traditional single-payload ADCs Shows strong tumor regression even in resistant models (preclinical data) Built with a next-gen linker platform to reduce toxicity & improve safety Trial Expansion & Regulatory Milestones Ongoing trials across Australia & the U.S. IND clearance received from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration Expansion planned in China (under review by National Medical Products Administration) Leadership Perspective As CEO Piers Ingram highlights, this milestone reflects their commitment to advancing multi-mechanism targeted therapies for cancer. CSO [Jerome Boyd-Kirkup](https://sg.linkedin.com/in/jeromeboydkirkup?trk=public_post-text) emphasized the superior anti-tumor activity and potential to outperform single-payload ADCs. CMO [Naomi Hunder](https://www.linkedin.com/in/naomi-hunder-b420525?trk=public_post-text) noted that CLIO-8221 could offer new hope for patients resistant to existing HER2 therapies, with improved safety profiles. # AI-generated books flood shelves, sacrificing quality for speed

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

2w

Several book pundits have coined the term the “Sheinification of publishing” to explain the increasing number of poorly written, AI slop-filled books onto our bookshelves. The theory is like fast fashion, which often sacrifices quality to churn out clothing quickly to meet trends, publishing is recently quick to publish books fueled by celebrity or popular tropes, in lieu of literary quality and polished storytelling. As an author, this is deeply infuriating. As a reader, even more so. Why is the assumption that the reading public prefers surface-level storytelling as opposed to rich prose and deeper narratives? Headlines below of books being called out for being generated by AI will continue, as they should. A well-written book is not something that’s churned out like a t-shirt. An author’s output from time in a chair, fingers on keyboard, ruminating in story, cannot be replicated by a machine spitting out em-dash filled sentences. I say bring back the "Hermesification" of books—high quality, exceptional craftsmanship, artisanal quality products by real humans whose attention to detail is unparalleled.

I'm afraid this is going to be something normal soon (as much as I hate it), but until there's a way for publishers to know if a manuscript is human-written or AI-written, honesty goes a long way

Each year, our Annual Leadership Awards recognize colleagues who turn ambition into impact for our customers and communities. Our 2026 Chester Fisher Commercial Excellence Award recognizes Duane Talhouk, whose 37-year career has been defined by one constant: putting customers first. Named after the founder of Fisher Scientific, this award honors exceptional customer focus and leadership. Duane brings both to life every day by building trusted partnerships that create long-term value while coaching teams to achieve more together. “What makes Duane’s leadership exceptional is his ability to combine deep customer understanding with the discipline to build scalable, long-term solutions,” says Thermo Fisher’s Lisa Witte. After 6 years at Thermo Fisher Scientific, I was laid off, and while it's bittersweet, I'm feeling grateful and ready for what's next! During this time, I had the opportunity to collaborate with incredible teams on projects including: - Bioinformatics leadership, data analysis, algorithm development and troubleshooting for the recently launched SwiftArray Studio, the next-generation microarray instrument, assay, and arrays. - Working hands-on with pioneering biobanking customers on some of the largest genomic datasets in the world. - Founding mentorship programs and community initiatives that are leadership-endorsed and still ongoing. I'm now looking for my next opportunity and I'd love your help. A bit about my background: - Staff Bioinformatics Scientist with 6+ years embedded in the product development process, from early R&D through verification, validation, and launch. - Built data pipelines, sensor and image quality algorithms, and QC dashboards. - Comfortable integrating AI and LLM tools into day-to-day workflows to accelerate analysis, automate reporting, and move faster from data to insight. - Deep interest in health tech and wearables, with personal projects tracking and analyzing my own biometric data. - A career spent bridging science, engineering, and product teams in cross-functional environments. 📍 EMIM 2026 is one week away and our team is looking forward to connecting with the molecular imaging community in Ljubljana. [EMIT Imaging](https://www.linkedin.com/company/emitimaging?trk=public_post-text) will be at Booth 304, sharing how 3D Cryo-Fluorescence Tomography (CFT) complements PET, SPECT, and optical imaging by providing co-registered anatomical and molecular information in a single dataset. CFT is an ex vivo whole-body imaging platform delivering sub-organ level resolution (~20–55 μm) with nM sensitivity, generating 3D datasets where anatomical images can be directly overlaid with fluorescence signal to precisely localize biodistribution and protein expression. For EMIM attendees, CFT can help: • Extend in vivo PET and SPECT findings with detailed 3D biodistribution • Further characterize tracer distribution within organs and tissues • Enable multiplex fluorescence studies in a single sample • Localize signal in small structures such as marrow and lymphatics • Strengthen translational imaging workflows with complementary datasets 📄Be sure to check out the multiple posters and talks taking place at EMIM that were enabled with CFT: [https://hubs.ly/Q043SgTW0](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Fhubs%2Ely%2FQ043SgTW0&urlhash=2J6-&trk=public_post-text)📅 Book time with [Ciara Finucane](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/ciara-finucane-b4669345?trk=public_post-text) in advance: [https://hubs.ly/Q043Sf7w0](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Fhubs%2Ely%2FQ043Sf7w0&urlhash=sSpS&trk=public_post-text) [#EMIM2026](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Femim2026&trk=public_post-text) [#EMIM](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Femim&trk=public_post-text) [#MolecularImaging](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2F

Stop Looking at My Twitter!Today, at the office, I was asked about Twitter. And you know, had I been asked last week, I would have simply looked at the person inquiring, dumbfounded, and told her that it was probably inappropriate for her to be talking to me about my twitter, and such conversation could possibly be considered sexual harassment.But to my credit, or discredit, I actually knew what she was talking about, and it had nothing to do with my body. Twitter, I have learned recently, is a micro-blog. (Oh, thanks Rob, that clears it up!) In fact, when one participates in Twitter (known as "tweeting") they are simply answering the question: "What are you doing" in 140 characters or less. You find folks you want to "follow" which consists of getting their updated tweets, and then others choose to follow you. That truly is all it is. So why all the rave?Well, I can't really explain it. In fact, I was conned into joining by two of my friends just last week while I was in Los Angeles; but ever since then, I cannot seem to pull myself away from the concept. Some of my more recent posts have included: * "Tonight I embark on my first parenting class...breast feeding 101. I am forecasting a 90% chance of awkward mixed with partly uncomfortable" * "Getting ready for my basketball game tonight...high-tops, gym shorts, jersey, and ibuprofens" * "Just arrived at LAX for our midnight flight; who flies this late?" * "

I have now had 4 chikfila sandwiches in 2 days...so i guess the diet is going well"

So shortly after joining, I quickly began thinking, who is going to want to follow me and why do I have this Twitter Account? Seriously, isn't it time for me to grow up? I mean what am I going to write everyday: "At work today," "Still at work," "Heading home from work." Riveting, right?But then, not a moment too soon, I came across an Oklahoma Bar Association article that spoke about the great potential Twitter can have for professionals if used appropriately. As for the content of the article, well, I basically skimmed it, but just the fact that it was there gave me the confidence I needed to tweet away.So for all those skeptics, don't knock it until you try it. And for those of you bold enough to join, come follow me on twitter. You never know, you may just catch me being full of twit.

Roan Joseph Bronstein is the eldest son of Sharon Stone and [Phil Bronstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phil_Bronstein). He was born on May 22, 2000, in Texas. he was adopted shortly after birth and raised between Los Angeles and San Francisco. Later in life, he legally added “Stone” to his name, becoming Roan Joseph Bronstein Stone to honor both of his parents. As of 2026, he is building his own path as a media entrepreneur, actor, and creative director.

## Roan Joseph Bronstein’s Early Years

Roan’s life started in a very emotional and meaningful way. He was born in Texas to a young biological mother and was adopted by Sharon Stone and Phil Bronstein about a week later. His adoption came after Sharon had gone through several miscarriages, which makes his arrival into the family even more special.

But his childhood was not completely smooth. When his parents divorced in 2004, things became complicated. Their separation later turned into a public custody battle. By 2008, a court decided that Roan would live mainly with his father in San Francisco. Because of this, he grew up between San Francisco and Los Angeles, moving between two very different lifestyles.

Even with these challenges, he stayed close to both parents. In 2019, he made a powerful personal decision. He added “Stone” to his name. This showed that he valued both his mother and father equally, despite everything that happened in the past.

Education played a big role in shaping him. Instead of going straight into acting, he followed his passion for cooking. He trained at the Institute of Culinary Education in Pasadena, where he became a skilled chef. At one point, he even thought about joining the army. But in the end, his love for creativity pulled him in another direction. Later, he also trained in acting at the Ivana Chubbuck Studio, one of the top acting schools.

Sheery Hall: I Joined Twitter

I have to laugh at myself. When Twitter began taking off in its popularity I was one that smirked and shook my head about it. So was my husband. We both thought that it was 'just another social site' like MySpace or Facebook. I did not anticipate the powerhouse Twitter would be in regard to professional networking. It seems that every where I turn - there's Twitter. As a writer (aspiring for publication) the opportunity to create name recognition is something I can not pass up. Essentially, Twitter is free advertising for me. Will I follow my family, friends, authors and bands that I like? Of course I will. There's no reason not to do so. So, feel free to laugh at me as I am laughing at myself. And here's [my link](http://www.twitter.com/SheeryHall) to Twitter if you are interested.

Confusing offers kill outbound… Outbound interrupts busy people. You have about five seconds to matter. If the value is unclear, the message dies instantly. Most teams try to explain too much. Multiple problems. Multiple outcomes. Multiple promises. The reader has to think. And thinking kills replies. Strong outbound removes friction. The offer must be obvious fast. Here’s the simple way to build it: - Define one painful problem. - Show the outcome they want and the unique way you solve it. - Give one clear next step. Nothing else. If the reader has to decode the message, the pipeline slows down. Clear offers move fast. Confusing offers stall everything. That’s why simplicity wins in outbound. The faster someone understands you, the faster they respond. _________________________________________ I run Boost Hero ([boosthero.co](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http%3A%2F%2Fboosthero%2Eco&urlhash=T-qF&trk=public_post-text)), where we build Revenue Engineering systems for predictable B2B growth. I share weekly insights on outbound, GTM systems, and pipeline economics. Follow Sergio Perea to learn more.

Prevention or Treatment? Why Big pHARMa is in business today. Prevention- removing a carcinogen from the water supply or banning a toxic food additive costs a company money and generates $0 in medical revenue. Treatment- A single patient can represent $500,000+ in revenue over several years. Ahh, jackpot. In the case of Pfizer, following the decline of COVID-19 vaccine sales, Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla pivoted the entire company toward cancer. He recently described the company's $43 billion acquisition of Seagen as a "bold move to tackle critical areas of unmet need." Analysts note that Pfizer is moving away from primary care (keeping people healthy) and toward "specialty oncology." Why? Because oncology drugs have some of the highest profit margins in the world and are shielded from the "patent cliffs" that kill off cheaper medications. In the case of Merck, CEO Rob Davis on a recent call highlighted that the company has line of sight to $70 billion in commercial opportunity by the mid-2030s. He stated, "Today, our business is anchored by an important set of commercial products that address critical unmet needs... representing the majority of our $70 billion of non-risk-adjusted commercial opportunity." Translation: Merck is explicitly counting on a steady stream of sick people to double their revenue from their current blockbuster, Keytruda. For Gilead, the biggest mistake they ever made was to make a drug that cured Hep C. Their sales subsequently plummeted. They'll never make that mistake again. And it goes on. These and other companies view the rising cancer rate not as a crisis to be solved by prevention, but as a growth driver. They are there to deliver SHAREHOLDER VALUE, and that means they LOVE CANCER. This is why nearly 4 in 10 Americans will be diagnosed with cancer. Yes, Four in Ten. And they are licking their chops at the prospect of that number r

[Callio Therapeutics](https://www.linkedin.com/company/calliotx?trk=public_post-text) Advances ADC Innovation: First Patient Dosed in Phase I Trial of CLIO-8221 The future of cancer treatment is evolving; and Callio Therapeutics is taking a bold step forward. The company has successfully dosed the first patient in its Phase I clinical trial of CLIO-8221, a first-in-class dual-payload antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting HER2-expressing solid tumors. What makes CLIO-8221 different? Combines two powerful mechanisms: Topo1 inhibitor + ATR inhibitor Designed to overcome resistance seen in traditional single-payload ADCs Shows strong tumor regression even in resistant models (preclinical data) Built with a next-gen linker platform to reduce toxicity & improve safety Trial Expansion & Regulatory Milestones Ongoing trials across Australia & the U.S. IND clearance received from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration Expansion planned in China (under review by National Medical Products Administration) Leadership Perspective As CEO Piers Ingram highlights, this milestone reflects their commitment to advancing multi-mechanism targeted therapies for cancer. CSO [Jerome Boyd-Kirkup](https://sg.linkedin.com/in/jeromeboydkirkup?trk=public_post-text) emphasized the superior anti-tumor activity and potential to outperform single-payload ADCs. CMO [Naomi Hunder](https://www.linkedin.com/in/naomi-hunder-b420525?trk=public_post-text) noted that CLIO-8221 could offer new hope for patients resistant to existing HER2 therapies, with improved safety profiles. | Marjorie Scardino left Pearson in 2012 |
| --- |

It hasn't felt like a great year for women at the very top of British business. There have been some outstanding commercial results - Louise Makin at BTG and Kate Swann at WH Smith stand out - but the number of women CEOs has fallen. [Marjorie Scardino](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/business-19811670) left Pearson in late 2012 and [Cynthia Carroll](http://www.independent.co.uk/news/business/news/cynthia-carroll-rough-ride-ends-for-the-outsider-at-anglo-american-8488459.html) Anglo-America in February. [Kate Swann](http://www.whsmithplc.co.uk/about_whsmith/directors/) has announced she will leave WH Smith in June this year after almost 10 years in which she has transformed the company's fortunes. These departures reduce the number of FTSE 350 companies led by women to 11, just 3% of the total ...

1. [Louise Makin](http://www.btgplc.com/about-us/leadership-team) BTG
2. [Angela Ahrendts](http://www.burberryplc.com/about_burberry/directors-and-management/angela-ahrendts) Burberry
3. [Dido Harding](http://www.talktalkgroup.com/investors/corporate-governance/board-of-directors.aspx) Talk Talk
4. [Carolyn McCall](http://corporate.easyjet.com/about-easyjet/our-management/board-of-directors.aspx) Easy Jet
5. [Ruby McGregor-Smith](http://www.mitie.com/investors/leadership) MITIE
6. [Harriet Green](http://www.guardian.co.uk/business/2012/may/24/harriet-green-thomas-cook) Thomas Cook
7. [Dorothy Thompson](http://www.draxgroup.plc.uk/aboutus/theboard/?id=1959) DRAX
8. [Caroline Banszky](http://www.lawdeb.com/investment-trust/team-members/caroline-banszky?page=) Law Debenture
9. [Alison Cooper](http://www.imperial-tobacco.com/index.asp?page=357) Imperial Tobacco
10. [Lyn Fordham](http://www.svgcapital.com/capital/board/) SVG Capital
11. [Katherine Garrett-Cox](http://investor.alliancetrust.co.uk/ati/investorrelations/garrettcox.jsp) Alliance Trust

There are certainly some very able women serving as executive directors and particularly CFOs of FTSE 350 companies but there numbers are increasing very slowly. The % of women executive directors has increased from just 5.5% to 5.8% since the publication of the Davies Report in 2011. Only 8% of executive appointments over this period have been women. Unless we see significant growth in the number of women executive directors, it is very hard to see from where the next generation of women CEOs will come. If you enjoyed this post you might also like ... [The Mums Who Are Beating the FTSE](http://womenonboard.blogspot.co.uk/2012/07/mums-who-are-beating-ftse.html) [Holding the Purse Strings ... [telli](https://de.linkedin.com/company/tellitechnologies?trk=public_post-text) now integrates natively with Salesforce ☎️ Our most requested integration is now live. Salesforce is where most of our customers manage their contacts, and until today, syncing that data meant uploading CSVs or building a custom integration. Today we fixed that. Here's how it works: - Connect your Salesforce org via OAuth - Sync Salesforce Contacts and Leads into telli - Map Salesforce fields to your telli contact properties - Filter which Salesforce records should be synced - Soon: Activity logging and write-back from telli to Salesforce Your agents now work from live Salesforce data at all times. Goldman Sachs has just added another important voice to the debate on AI and jobs and, again, the picture is far more nuanced than the headlines suggest. Their latest research estimates that AI could expose 300 million jobs globally to automation, with tasks accounting for around 25% of all US work hours potentially affected. That is a huge number and the detail matters. Goldman Sachs is not predicting some overnight collapse in employment. In its base case, AI adoption plays out over around 10 years, with 6–7% of workers displaced during that transition. Even then, the projected impact on unemployment is relatively modest if the shift happens gradually. At the same time, AI is already creating demand elsewhere. In the US alone, around 500,000 net new jobs are expected to be needed by 2030 to support rising power demand. Construction jobs linked to data centre build-out have already increased by 216,000 since 2022. And beyond infrastructure, Goldman points to growth in AI-skilled roles, new specialist occupations, and entirely new categories of work that emerge as productivity and incomes rise. That’s why I keep coming back to the same point: The biggest risk right now is not simply that AI removes jobs. It’s that employers make short-term hiring decisions based on fear of what AI might do, rather than evidence of what is actually happening. I’ve written recently about PwC increasing graduate hiring, about application volumes surging, and about the urgent need for more pathways into work for young people. I’ve also argued that the real danger is employers hollowing out early careers pipelines before the labour market has had time to adjust. This Goldman Sachs research strengthens that case. Yes, AI will reshape work. Yes, some roles, especially in knowledge and creative sectors, will change significantly. An

[Goldman Sachs](https://www.linkedin.com/company/goldman-sachs?trk=public_post-text) has launched G-European Credit ELTIF, its first evergreen product under the ELTIF 2.0 regime, extending its European direct lending strategy into a structure designed for broader investor access. → The new ELTIF extends Goldman’s evergreen European private credit strategy launched in October 2023. → Goldman said that strategy had grown to more than $8bn including leverage as of March 4, 2026, making it one of the largest evergreen private credit funds in Europe. → The new vehicle will mainly invest in senior secured private credit. # Goldman Sachs report misses AI's true economic impact

[](https://www.linkedin.com/in/matt-caron?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[👨🏼💻 Matt Caron](https://www.linkedin.com/in/matt-caron?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)

4d Edited

- [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fmatt-caron_i-was-talking-to-a-friend-about-that-goldman-activity-7440433990290857985-K0DT&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

I was talking to a friend about that Goldman Sachs report. The one saying AI’s contribution to US economic growth is “basically zero.” And we both had the same reaction: they’re not wrong, they’re just measuring the wrong thing. Like when I use AI to save 3 hours on a task, I don’t clock out early. I pour that time into harder problems, better work, stuff I never had the bandwidth for before. None of that shows up in a GDP report. But it’s very real. Multiply that across millions of people and you’ve got this massive invisible productivity wave that the macro data just can’t see yet. So those who fade the economic benefits of LLMs have to change how they measure. It’s a new world

[Accenture](https://ie.linkedin.com/company/accenture?trk=public_post-text) and [Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text) have launched a forward-deployed engineering practice, bringing thousands of AI-skilled engineers to work directly inside client organisations, designed to take AI from idea to production in days rather than months. [Manish Sharma](https://www.linkedin.com/in/manish-sharma-bbb1a1?trk=public_post-text), Chief Strategy and Services Officer at Accenture, on what actually drives AI value, "AI value does not come from technology access but from the ability to convert it into sustained business impact. Enterprise AI succeeds when strategy and engineering operate as one." [Nick Parker](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nick-parker-0007aa46?trk=public_post-text), EVP and Chief Business Officer at Microsoft, on what clients are asking for, "What customers are asking us for is a clear path to business outcomes powered by AI. Microsoft's end-to-end AI platform combined with Accenture's industry expertise will enable the FDE crews to quickly create the highest quality outcomes for customers." The model is deliberate in its division of labour. Microsoft provides the platform and frontier AI capabilities. Accenture leads change management, process redesign, industry workflows and global deployment. Joint teams co-innovate directly with clients using Microsoft's Frontier Suite and proven accelerators, working shoulder-to-shoulder rather than handing off between vendors. The diagnosis underlying the launch is the right one. Most enterprise AI initiatives stall not because the technology is unavailable, but because the engineering expertise needed to operationalise it at the right point in the right workflow simply isn't there. ### IRS Overreach In Action With Leighanne Lafrenz Nickle

April 2, 2026

In this episode of the Tax Rep Network Podcast, Eric Green is joined by [Leighanne Lafrenz Nickle](https://www.linkedin.com/in/leighanne-lafrenz-nickle-cpa-3341069b/), CPA, for a lively discussion on ERC claims, IRS due diligence audits, preparer penalties, and the growing problem of IRS overreach. Drawing from real cases and frontline experience, they break down what tax professionals need to know about refund deadlines, documentation, aggressive auditors, and why strong representation matters more than ever. If you handle tax controversy work, this episode is packed with practical insight you can use right now.

Want to contact Leighanne? Email her at: [leighanne@lafrenznicklecpa.com](mailto:leighanne@lafrenznicklecpa.com).

### Listen to the podcast here

IRS Overreach, ERC Chaos, and What Tax Pros Need to Know Now

The IRS is turning up the heat—and for tax professionals navigating Employee Retention Credit (ERC) claims and preparer audits, the landscape is becoming increasingly complex and aggressive. In a recent discussion, seasoned tax professionals unpacked what’s really happening behind the scenes, and why many believe the IRS has crossed the line from enforcement into overreach.

### The ERC: From Relief to Risk

Originally designed as a lifeline during COVID-19, the ERC was meant to incentivize businesses to keep employees on payroll. It offered substantial refundable credits—up to $5,000 per employee in 2020 and $7,000 per employee per quarter in 2021. Eligibility hinged on either a significant drop in gross receipts or being impacted by government shutdown orders.

But now, years later, the IRS is aggressively auditing these claims. Thousands of businesses are receiving denial letters (Letter 105(c)), often with little room for negotiation. Taxpayers have just 30 days to request an appeal—and critically, only two years from the denial date to file a lawsuit. Miss that window, and the claim is lost forever.

### The Shutdown Debate: A Legal Battleground

One of the biggest areas of contention is what qualifies as a “government shutdown.” The IRS has taken a narrow view, relying heavily on Notice 2021-20, which states that only explicit federal or state shutdown orders count. Guidance from agencies like OSHA or the CDC? According to the IRS, those don’t qualify.

That position is now being challenged in court. A key case—Carrolton of Dunn v. United States—argues that businesses were materially impacted by COVID-related restrictions even if they were technically allowed to remain open. For example,

##### IBM Fellow and Quantum Pioneer Charles H. Bennett Receives A.M. Turing Award

102,575 followers

3w Edited

Congratulations to [IBM](https://www.linkedin.com/company/ibm?trk=public_post-text) Fellow and quantum pioneer [Charles H. Bennett](https://www.linkedin.com/in/charles-h-bennett-15898882?trk=public_post-text) on being named a co‑recipient of the 2025 ACM A.M. Turing Award by the [ACM, Association for Computing Machinery](https://www.linkedin.com/company/association-for-computing-machinery?trk=public_post-text)—the seventh IBM‑affiliated researcher to receive this honor, and the first recognized for contributions to quantum information science: [https://lnkd.in/eDmrZrM2](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeDmrZrM2&urlhash=X57i&trk=public_post-text) Sharing the award with longtime collaborator Gilles Brassard of the [Université de Montréal](https://ca.linkedin.com/school/universite-de-montreal/?trk=public_post-text), the citation recognizes their foundational work that helped ignite the “quantum revolution,” advance the field of quantum information science, and reshape how researchers understand computation, communication, and the nature of information itself. Across more than five decades at [IBM Research](https://www.linkedin.com/showcase/ibm-research/?trk=public_post-text), Bennett explored the deep connections between computation and the laws of physics, pioneering ways to harness quantum behavior at the smallest scales to process and transmit information in ways classical computers cannot. His work on logical reversibility of computation (1973), quantum cryptography (BB84, the first quantum key distribution protocol, 1984), quantum teleportation (1993), and entanglement distillation (1996) helped lay the scientific foundation for concepts that shaped and continue to enable the progress of quantum computing today. You can still find Bennett at the IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center in Yorktown Heights, New York. When reflecting on the years in which he achieved the above breakthroughs, he said: “IBM was an ideal place to do this kind of research because you had people working on the fundamental physics of computing and hardware, and in the same building, people focused on the mathematics of computing. # Lou Aversano’s Post

- [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Flouaversano_ibm-and-ogilvy-end-32-year-creative-partnership-activity-7440920265414397953-UCBk&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

The news of the IBM & Ogilvy relationship ending after 30+ years has sparked a wave of reflection from an extraordinary group of people around the world. Much will be said about the consistent body of great work delivered year after year with precision and impact. Work that helped rejuvenate a great brand and global enterprise at a time when many were calling for its breakup. Much will be written about the many “firsts” the teams created together: a New York Times front-page–worthy global consolidation; defining the internet era through an integrated e-business platform that positioned the web as a business tool, not a browsing fad; and simple, powerful films that humanized technology - like Czech speaking nuns singing the praises of IBM software airing on Sunday NFL games. Lest we forget the 60+ unique spots on the Masters to showcase IBM’s breadth of impact or the weekly 600-word, thought provoking print ads during the financial crisis which argued that a mandate for change is a mandate for smart. And one of the most important product demonstrations ever - introducing the world to AI through Watson’s victory on Jeopardy in 2011. The list goes on and on and on. But what I will remember most are the people. The teams across Ogilvy, IBM, and many other partner agencies, who operated as one. People who respected, listened to, and challenged each other. People who bonded so closely it was often impossible to distinguish client from agency. The mission was too important for anything less and it created a sense of family. And like any family, there were ups and downs. But we stayed together because we knew we were better together than apart. IBM Aquires Confluent To Boost Real Time AI Data Access Mar 17, 2026 Big moves are happening in the AI space, and [#IBM](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fibm&trk=public_post-text) is making one of them. The company has completed a $11 billion acquisition of [#Confluent](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fconfluent&trk=public_post-text), a platform known for helping organizations manage and stream large amounts of data in real time. Beyond a tech dea, it points to something much bigger. Today, [#AI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fai&trk=public_post-text) systems rely heavily on data. Not just any data, but clean, organized, and real-time data that can power accurate insights and decisions. Without this foundation, even the most advanced AI models struggle to perform effectively. AI success isn't just about algorithms—it’s about data infrastructure—and IBM is on the lead. The significance: By strengthening its data capabilities, IBM is positioning itself to build smarter AI systems and more reliable enterprise solutions. Better [#data](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdata&trk=public_post-text) flow means: faster decision-making, more responsive AI agents, and more scalable AI applications - Written by Joseph Alu Appendix: Rob Thomas, Senior Vice President, IBM Software and Chief Commercial Officer, said: “Together, IBM and Confluent give enterprises the foundation for a new operating model - one where AI runs on live data, drives decisions in real time, and delivers value at scale.” Data Streaming: The process of continuously sending and processing data in real time instead of storing it first. # IBM Hires 3x More Entry-Level Jobs Amid Industry Layoffs

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

 

 

[](https://www.linkedin.com/in/warrenwangfin?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Warren Wang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/warrenwangfin?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) 

 1w 

 

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fwarrenwangfin_every-tech-company-in-2026-is-cutting-junior-activity-7440004327919972352-VGAi&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

Every tech company in 2026 is cutting junior roles. But IBM is doing the opposite. They want to hire 3x more for entry-level jobs: While Block fired 4,000 people. While Atlassian cut 1,600. While Meta plans to cut 20%. While 37% of companies say they'll replace entry-level jobs with AI. IBM's CHRO said something every CEO needs to hear: "If you stop hiring juniors today, you won't have mid-level managers in five years. And poaching them will cost you a 30% premium." So IBM rewrote every entry-level job description. Not around coding or tasks AI can do. But around customer engagement, product thinking, and judgment. The stuff AI can't do yet. They're hiring for the gap between ... what AI delivers and what humans decide to do with it. Everyone else is optimizing for 2026. IBM is hiring for 2031. The companies cutting the deepest right now might be the ones paying the most in talent premiums five years from now To understand how the top CHROs think, I created [The CHRO Office](https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-chro-office?trk=public_post-text). A place where 11,000+ CHROs get the AI playbooks, the strategies, and the honest conversations nobody else is having about this role.

🚨 **Bitcoin ETFs Just Broke Records—But Here's What Nobody's Talking About** 🚨 The past 4 weeks have been WILD for [#Bitcoin](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbitcoin&trk=public_post-text) ETF trading. We're talking $31.6 billion in daily volume on March 2 alone—the highest ever recorded. But here's the plot twist: massive volume ≠ massive buying. 📊 **What's Actually Happening:** • **Record Gross Volume, Mixed Net Flows** — Bitcoin ETFs hit 4 all-time high trading days in March, but net flows on March 18-19 were actually NEGATIVE ($219.5M and $217.24M outflows). Translation: Institutions are repositioning, not necessarily bullish. • **Institutional Repositioning at Scale** — [#BlackRock](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fblackrock&trk=public_post-text)'s iShares Bitcoin Trust dominated flows, absorbing $199.4M in Bitcoin purchases on March 17 alone. This isn't retail FOMO—it's sophisticated capital adjusting positions during volatility. • **Market Depth Over Direction** — The surge in ETF volume reflects how mature regulated crypto investment vehicles have become. Institutions can now move massive positions without touching custody, tightening supply and amplifying demand impact. • **Macro Headwinds Didn't Stop the Action** — Despite Bitcoin declining from $74K to below $70K, ETF volumes SURGED. Geopolitical tensions, Fed uncertainty, and gold's 5% drop didn't scare institutions away—they doubled down on repositioning. • **Ethereum & Altcoins Following Suit** — [#Ethereum](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fethereum&trk=public_po

But for the class of 2026, that transition may be rockier than ever, with BlackRock CEO Larry Fink issuing a warning that the promise of a four-year degree as a pathway to a stable career is beginning to crack. [BlackRock](https://www.linkedin.com/company/blackrock?trk=public_post-text) CEO [Larry Fink](https://www.linkedin.com/in/laurencefink?trk=public_post-text) has warned that the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) tools could create a 'crisis' for the graduating Gen Z college students. Speaking at BlackRock's 2026 Infrastructure Summit, Fink stated that the technology could wipe out the entry-level roles that traditionally kickstart corporate careers, potentially leading to record-high unemployment. "When this year's college graduates enter the workforce, we could see the highest unemployment rate among them in years, even without a recession," said Fink as per Fortune. "The speed at which AI is changing, we're not adapting our society fast enough. Really, post World War II, the pathway to a white-collar job was a college education, and AI is going to disrupt many of those types of jobs." Fink highlighted that the usual pathway of completing a four-year college before transitioning to an entry-level job was becoming just one of several viable paths to success. “AI is going to create many jobs and we're not prepared as a society to fulfill those jobs,” Fink said. “And to me, this is a crisis.”

I love this chart. It’s simple, but it has me feeling pretty optimistic about alpha generation these days. Why? Across 2023-2025, just over a mere quarter of S&P 500 companies outperformed the index. Upshot: A tough hand dealt to active managers. YTD, outperformance has broadened to more than half of S&P 500 companies, above the historic average. Upshot: A better opportunity set for active managers. It’s a sentiment I shared earlier this week on a call attended by 1,000+ BlackRock clients. Recent market broadening reinforces my view that active management is more critical today as the opportunities to outpace the market rest not with a small handful but with a growing number of stocks. The skill is in finding them. Last week, BlackRock and Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP) convened leaders across finance, policy, technology, and labor in Washington, D.C. — including Secretary Chris Wright, Governor Gretchen Whitmer, Ruth Porat, Dina Powell McCormick, and Sam Altman — to discuss how investing in American infrastructure can act as a catalyst for job creation, economic growth, and the development of American talent. Throughout our conversations, a few key themes stood out: • Power demand is accelerating. As AI scales, the need for generation, grid upgrades, and enabling infrastructure is becoming a multi‑year investment imperative. • Reliability and security matter more than ever. These factors are increasingly core to underwriting long‑duration infrastructure assets. • Workforce development is critical. Rising demand for skilled labor means investment must go hand‑in‑hand with training and pathways to durable, good‑paying jobs. That’s why talent and labor are central to the discussion. Through The BlackRock Foundation’s $100 million Future Builders initiative, we’re investing in pathways to high‑growth careers for more Americans, while helping ensure communities have the workforce needed to turn investment into infrastructure. These dynamics are already shaping client conversations across the US. At BlackRock, we’re focused on helping investors navigate what this next phase of infrastructure buildout means for portfolios today – and for the workers and communities powering long-term growth.

Skills based hiring and talent development strategies are still a big goal across industries but when I ask folks, where are you with it? The answer is most often "we started from the bottom, now we're a little bit above the bottom." Wait. No, that's from the meme in today's newsletter, actually. But the answer isn't far from that typically. It's going slow and now the skills landscape is shifting faster than we've ever seen before, making the foundational tasks behind a skills based strategy harder to achieve. One of the first sessions I attended at [#Transform](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Ftransform&trk=public_post-text) was a session about this topic. Some visits stay with you longer than expected. Two weeks on, I’m still thinking about my time at our [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.linkedin.com/company/bristol-myers-squibb?trk=public_post-text) Research site in Sevilla, Spain. The Center for Innovation and Translational Research in Europe (or CITRE, as we call it) has been a vital part of our Research organization for 15 years. Our CITRE scientists help bring innovation to life by: 🧬 Advancing patient‑centric translational models and biomarkers 📈 Enabling data‑driven decisions across more than 60% of global early hematology oncology research programs 🧑🔬 Serving as a gateway to European academic collaboration and talent From poster sessions to flash talks — including two excellent Research Town Hall presentations by [Hannah Pliner](https://es.linkedin.com/in/hannah-pliner-67779349?trk=public_post-text) and [Maria Dolores Jimenez Nuñez](https://es.linkedin.com/in/maria-dolores-jimenez-nu%C3%B1ez-8902394a?trk=public_post-text)— I was struck by our CITRE scientists’ passion, ingenuity, and dedication to patients. If you’d like to learn more, I encourage you to read the El Mundo article linked in the comments, which perfectly captures the value CITRE brings to our Research organization and our commitment to breakthrough science.

The report recognises the critical role Australia’s health and medical life sciences sector can play in driving productivity, strengthening economic resilience and enhancing global competitiveness at a time of accelerating international investment in innovation and critical industries. We particularly welcome the recognition of “health and medical” as the first of six National Innovation Pillars, acknowledging our sector’s vital role in driving domestic innovation, improving health outcomes and strengthening Australia’s economic competitiveness. This type of cross-portfolio partnership with industry will improve policy coherence across government and support strategic investment and productivity gains. We thank our partners at [Medicines Australia](https://au.linkedin.com/company/medicines-australia?trk=public_post-text) and [MTPConnect](https://au.linkedin.com/company/mtp-connect?trk=public_post-text) and members [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.linkedin.com/company/bristol-myers-squibb?trk=public_post-text) and [UCB](https://be.linkedin.com/company/ucb-pharma?trk=public_post-text) for their joint advocacy on this reform. Both peak bodies support the Report’s first recommendation — to establish a National Innovation Council reporting directly to the Prime Minister, and the Minister for Industry and Innovation and Minister for Science. - 

 

Breakthrough in Metastatic Breast Cancer Treatment: Pfizer's Atirmociclib Phase 2 Results Are In. This week, Pfizer announced that atirmociclib, an investigational next-generation CDK4 inhibitor, met the primary endpoint of the Phase 2 FOURLIGHT-1 trial with a 40% reduction in the risk of disease progression or death in HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. Here's why this matters: ✅ Hazard ratio of 0.60 (p=0.0007) — statistically significant and clinically meaningful ✅ 264 patients enrolled across 14 countries ✅ 90%+ had progressed within 3 months of prior CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy — a notoriously difficult population ✅ Only 6.4% discontinued due to adverse events — a strong tolerability signal ✅ Consistent benefit across ALL prespecified subgroups What makes atirmociclib different? Unlike existing CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib), atirmociclib selectively targets CDK4, with the goal of improving efficacy and reducing myelosuppressive side effects. Pfizer is now accelerating into: → Phase 3 in first-line metastatic breast cancer (ongoing) → Phase 2 neoadjuvant study in early-stage disease (data pending) For the ~500,000 people living with metastatic breast cancer worldwide, results like these represent more than statistics — they represent time. Full analysis → [https://lnkd.in/gxGuR23q](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgxGuR23q&urlhash=agf1&trk=public_post-text) [#BreastCancer](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbreastcancer&trk=public_post-text) [#Oncology](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Foncology&trk=public_po

PFIZER MED DESIGN PUBLICATION: An orphan GPCR with some chemical matter, thanks to High Throughput Screening and now published in this J Med Chem paper led by Ethan Fisher and [Yuan Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yuan-zhang-700b6817?trk=public_post-text). One million compounds screened identified an ~8uM GPR61 lead. This starting point was subsequently optimized to <10nM potency with tool compounds good enough for peripheral and CNS penetrant use. Oh, and a cryo-EM structure to assist with compound design for good measure too! This was a team effort between Medicine Design and the Internal Medicine Research Unit. There's a long list of Med Design authors past and present to namecheck here, so here we go! Ethan Fisher, [Yuan Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yuan-zhang-700b6817?trk=public_post-text), [Annemarie Schmitt](https://www.linkedin.com/in/annemarie-schmitt-a0718b14b?trk=public_post-text), [Jamie Tuttle](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jamie-tuttle-62916711?trk=public_post-text), [Ray Unwalla](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rayunwalla?trk=public_post-text), [Gabrielle Lovett](https://www.linkedin.com/in/gabrielle-lovett-3261209b?trk=public_post-text), [Bethany L Kormos PhD](https://www.linkedin.com/in/bethany-kormos-53805b4?trk=public_post-text), [Karen Coffman](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karen-coffman-b5126131?trk=public_post-text), Dahui Zhou, [Michael Moran](https://www.linkedin.com/in/michael-moran2020?trk=public_post-text), [Jade Williams, Ph.D.](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jade-c-williams?trk=public_post-text), Jun Xiao, [Erik LaChapelle](https://www.linkedin.com/in/erik-lachapelle-99957aa?trk=public_post-text), [Kimberly Stevens](https://www.linkedin.com/in/kimberly-stevens-69539030?trk=public_post-text), [Rebecca O'Connor](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rebecca-o-connor

I just called [WellsFargo.com](https://www.linkedin.com/company/wellsfargo-com?trk=public_post-text) because I tried to open another checking out for one of my businesses they already have accounts with. I filled out the request online 3 days ago. They called me and asked some questions and told me they would send me a Docusign with documents for the account. I’ve heard nothing since then. I’m a banking executive. You can open accounts instantly online with Plaid now. I called [WellsFargo.com](https://www.linkedin.com/company/wellsfargo-com?trk=public_post-text) tonight. I explain the story and ask for an update after holding for 10 minutes. After a lengthy verification process, I am told I need to be transferred to ‘business accounts.’ Another hold. Another verification. It’s been 30 minutes now. I explain the story and am then told I need to be transferred to ‘small business account opening.’ I voice measured, but concerned frustration. I am transferred anyway. Department is closed. [WellsFargo.com](https://www.linkedin.com/company/wellsfargo-com?trk=public_post-text) I have been a client since I was 18. I will be closing all my accounts on Monday. It shouldn’t be this hard to give my money. Deposits are the lifeblood of banks.

# AI Systems Evolve: MCP, A2A, and the Future of Agentic AI

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

Wells Fargo•717K followers

3w

AI systems are becoming protocol-driven systems. A lot of the current discussion frames this as MCP vs A2A. I think that misses the bigger point. These are not competing ideas in the long run. They solve different problems. MCP helps agents access tools, services, and structured context in a standardized way. A2A helps agents communicate with other agents, delegate tasks, and coordinate work across a distributed system. When you put them together, the picture becomes much more interesting. You start moving from isolated AI agents to real Agentic AI systems. That shift looks like this: Apps → Agents → Multi-Agent Systems → AI Infrastructure And once you get there, the real challenges begin: How does an agent identify itself? How do multiple agents coordinate safely? How do you trace execution across the whole system? How do you enforce guardrails, permissions, and policies? How do you observe failures in production? This is why I believe the future will not be built on rag/prompts alone. It will be built on orchestration, protocols, identity, observability, and execution layers that make AI systems reliable at scale. In simple terms: MCP = capability layer A2A = communication layer Together, they form the foundation for production-grade AI infrastructure. I rebuilt this idea visually to show how MCP, A2A, and a unified architecture fit together on the same page.
