# NHS AI Adoption: From Pilots to System-Level Change

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

3w

Recent conversations with [NHS Trust](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/nhs-trust?trk=public_post-text) leaders have made that pretty clear: the debate about whether AI belongs in the NHS is over. The question now is how to use it in ways that truly move the needle on outcomes. [A note on perspective: this post was written in collaboration with our Global GenAI Health Care Lead [Niraj Dalmia](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/nirajdalmia?trk=public_post-text)] There are already real reasons for optimism. Clinicians are making faster, more accurate decisions in time-critical pathways. Administrative load is coming down. In areas of acute workforce pressure, AI is creating headroom that simply didn't exist before. But point-in-time successes are not the same as system-level change, and that gap is where the NHS is currently stuck. The problem isn't a shortage of innovation. It's a delivery problem. Pilots stall before they scale. Procurement is fragmented, governance is duplicated, data isn't ready, and the organizational capacity to actually operationalize change is stretched thin. The places where AI has broken through share a recognizable set of conditions: clinical ownership, shared infrastructure, governance built in from the start, and a definition of success tied to patient pathway impact rather than technical performance. Getting from where the NHS is now to where it needs to be means changing the frame entirely. Less focus on interesting experiments, more focus on outcomes. Less single-trust adoption, more shared platforms and coordinated approaches. The shift, in short, is from asking "does this work?" to "how do we make this the norm?" London is better placed than almost anywhere to lead that shift. The density of NHS providers, clinical talent, research institutions and health innovation infrastructure in this city is unusual. Treated as a single ecosystem rather than a collection of competing organizations, London could become a global reference point for AI-enabled health care that is safe, equitable and built to last. AI is not a fix for everything the NHS is grappling with. But the opportunity in front of us is real, and it won't be unlocked by better algorithms. It will be unlocked by better execution.

Big news out of Abilene today! [Crusoe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/crusoe?trk=public_post-text) just announced a new 900 MW AI factory campus in Abilene, Texas, purpose-built to support [Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text) AI infrastructure. Combined with our existing footprint, the full Abilene site is now projected to reach 2.1 gigawatts of total capacity. From a policy perspective, this is exactly the kind of announcement that reflects how aligned infrastructure investment, energy policy, and economic development can be when all stakeholders are working in the same direction. A few things that stand out to me: 🔋 Energy-first design — the new campus includes a dedicated on-site power plant + BESS designed to enhance grid resilience, not strain it. This is how responsible AI infrastructure gets built. 💼 Real economic impact — thousands of construction jobs, hundreds of permanent positions, and a growing share of Taylor County and City of Abilene property tax revenue. West Texas is seeing the tangible benefits of this investment. ⚡ Speed that matters — Crusoe energized its first two Abilene buildings in under a year. That pace is unmatched in this industry, and it matters enormously as the U.S. races to lead on AI. As policymakers and regulators from Texas to Washington continue to grapple with questions about data center energy use, grid reliability, and local impact — Abilene is a strong proof point that these things don't have to be in conflict. Proud of the team and grateful for the partners — in Abilene, in Austin, and in D.C. — who helped make this possible. At Verizon, we see how AI is fundamentally changing the digital landscape and workforce. Our greatest responsibility is to use the power of our technology, our partnerships, and our people to help the communities we serve thrive in a connected world. I am thrilled to announce that Verizon is partnering with [Grow with Google](https://www.linkedin.com/showcase/grow-with-google/?trk=public_post_reshare-text) to offer the new Google AI Professional Certificate to the over 500,000 small businesses we support through our Small Business Digital Ready initiative at no cost. This best-in-class training is being made available to small businesses nationwide. Verizon is proud to go a step further. In addition to small businesses, we have expanded access to this certificate to our recent Verizon alumni as part of our Reskilling and Career Transition Fund. Our goal is to ensure that those who have left Verizon feel equipped for the long term, not just the immediate future. This 10-hour, self-paced program moves beyond theory, offering over 20 hands-on activities in “vibe coding”, data analysis, and content creation. Our goal is to move learners from basic concepts to true AI fluency. By providing this new tool, we hope to help build an AI-fluent workforce, and to help people unlock new opportunities faster. Join us in building a more responsible and inclusive AI workforce. Christina Hammock Koch, chi è la prima astronauta donna sulla luna

Vai al contenuto

MIGUEL J RODRIGUEZ CARRILLO//Getty Images

"L'equipaggio di [Artemis-2](https://www.elle.com/it/magazine/a70891429/missione-artemis-ii-viaggio-verso-la-luna/) rappresenta migliaia di persone che lavorano instancabilmente per portarci alle stelle. Questo è il loro equipaggio, questo è il nostro equipaggio, questo è l'equipaggio dell'umanità". Così diceva nel 2023 l'amministratore della Nasa Bill Nelson che, tenendo fede al credo E pluribus unum, manteneva [la promessa di portare una maggiore pluralità all'interno dei team](https://www.elle.com/it/magazine/storie-di-donne/a26807760/donne-famose-astronaute/). La Nasa si dimostrava pronta ad aprire la squadra a nuove leve di professionisti dello spazio, scelti per il loro talento e indipendentemente dal colore della pelle e dal genere.

NASA//Getty Images

Il team di Artemis II

## La prima donna a viaggiare oltre l'orbita terrestre bassa verso la luna

E di fatti Artemis 2, che è pronta a partire nelle prossime ore (il decollo è previsto non prima delle 00:24 di giovedì 2 aprile ora legale dell’Europa centrale) per un viaggio di 10 giorni intorno alla luna e ritorno sulla Terra, è un equipaggio composto per la prima volta anche da una donna, [Christina Koch](https://www.elle.com/it/magazine/women-in-society/a43502559/christina-koch-astronauta-luna/), e un uomo afroamericano, Victor Glover.

Koch, dunque, è pronta a diventare la prima donna in assoluto a viaggiare oltre l'orbita terrestre bassa verso la luna, in quello che sarà un momento fondamentale nella storia del volo spaziale. Il programma Apollo ha inviato 24 astronauti maschi sulla luna tra il 1968 e il 1972, 12 dei quali hanno camminato sulla sua superficie. L'imminente viaggio di Koch segna non solo un risultato personale, ma anche un salto simbolico in avanti più di 50 anni dopo l'ultimo sbarco sulla luna della NASA, ovvero quello dell'Apollo 17 nel 1972.

## La biografia di Christina Hammock Koch

Nata in Michigan nel 1979, Christina Koch si è laureata in ingegneria elettrica e fisica, e ha conseguito un master in scienze dell'energia elettrica conseguito nel 2002. Dopo gli studi, ha lavorato al Laboratory for High Energy Astrophysics del GSFC ed è stata docente al Montgomery College, dove ha insegnato in un laboratorio di fisica. Tra il 2004 e il 2007 è stata ricercatrice del Programma Antartico degli Stati Uniti d'America, completando anche una stagione invernale alla Base Amundsen-Scott e una stagione alla Base Palmer. Dal 2007 al 2009 ha continuato a lavorare allo sviluppo di strumenti scientifici spaziali al Laboratorio di fisica applicata dell'Università Johns Hopkins, contribuendo agli strumenti sullo studio delle radiazioni delle missioni Juno e Van Allen Probes.

SERGEI ILNITSKY//Getty Images

Dal 2010, Koch è tornata a svolgere lavoro scientifico sul campo durante delle missioni alla Base Palmer e diverse stagioni invernali alla Base Summit in

hi there!!! can you believe it's time for another video with me as a guest with [Emily](https://youtu.be/KIfYUQHQ1hs) on the Two Many Cards Video Series?! that's right! please be sure to check out [her blog](https://leiphartonart.blogspot.com/2019/06/two-many-cards-video-series-featuring-lawn-fawn.html) for all of the important information!

what fun! today we're featuring stamps, dies and stencils from [Lawn Fawn](https://www.lawnfawn.com/products/lets-toast)!! be sure to check out [my video on youtube](https://youtu.be/eJPmjdZLqLI) for all the giveaway information, too! We used the new [On the Beach stamps](https://www.lawnfawn.com/collections/new-arrivals/products/on-the-beach) and [dies](https://www.lawnfawn.com/products/on-the-beach-lawn-cuts/), [Hammock and Trees dies](https://www.lawnfawn.com/collections/new-arrivals/products/hammock-and-trees) and the [Sandy Beach Accents dies](https://www.lawnfawn.com/collections/new-arrivals/products/sandy-beach-accents)! I did lots of Distress Ink blending on both cards!

I also used the [Stitched Hillside Borders dies](https://www.lawnfawn.com/products/stitched-hillside-borders) to create the sandy shore on the card with the hammock! I used Vintage Photo and Fossilized Amber Distress Inks for both sandy shores. the sea/sky for hammock card was ink blended with Blueprint Sketch Peacock Feathers, Cracked Pistachio and Mermaid Lagoon. the sky on the circle card used Black Soot, Wilted Violet, Blueprint Sketch and Peacock Feathers.

all of the images were colored with Zig Clean Color Real Brush Markers and then die cut out! the cards themselves took a journey as far as design went...but they turned out so cute!

Hi everyone—and a warm welcome to those who’ve recently joined my network! I am Steve and I’m grateful to be connected with such a great group of professionals here on LinkedIn. For those I haven’t met yet, I wanted to take a moment to reintroduce myself. I began my professional journey serving in the United States Air Force, where I developed a strong foundation in leadership, discipline, and mission-focused teamwork, especially around Logistics, supply chain, and eventually talent acquisition & career counseling. That experience continues to shape how I approach challenges and opportunities. Following my military service, I transitioned to [Verizon](https://www.linkedin.com/company/verizon?trk=public_post-text), where I’ve had the opportunity to grow, adapt, and contribute in a fast-paced, ever-evolving industry. My journey there has been both rewarding and transformative, allowing me to build meaningful relationships and drive impact through innovation and customer-focused solutions within Talent Acquisition as part of their Military Hiring programs coupled with my brand advocacy and marketing campaign participation. I’m especially passionate about leadership development, providing value to my teammates, and helping others unlock their potential. Whether it’s mentoring, collaborating on new ideas, or navigating change, I enjoy connecting with people who are driven to grow and make a difference. If you’re new here, I’d love to learn more about you—what you’re working on, what inspires you, and how we might support each other. Looking forward to engaging with you all! Customer experience doesn’t improve because of one big technology decision. It only improves when organizations can seamlessly integrate technology, people, and empathy into their operations. In the latest episode of the [Stare Down the Bull PodCast](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stare-down-the-bull-podcast?trk=public_post_reshare-text), CX strategist [Chris Arnold](https://www.linkedin.com/in/chrisdotarnold?trk=public_post_reshare-text) shares what he’s learned after two decades leading customer experience at the enterprise level with [Verizon](https://www.linkedin.com/company/verizon?trk=public_post_reshare-text). One of the biggest mistakes companies make is treating CX like a binary choice: Automation or humans; digital or voice. But the future belongs to companies that integrate all of it. As Chris says, the goal is not replacing people; it's elevating them. In this episode, we discuss: ➡️ How AI is transforming contact centers ➡️ Why the “build vs. wait” mindset around AI is risky ➡️ The importance of breaking down CX silos across departments ➡️ Why empathy remains a competitive advantage (even in an automated world) If you're thinking about the future of CX and AI, you'll want to hear this conversation. When you see this photo do you feel like you know her? Do you feel like you would be in safe hands? A great headshot can make a huge difference if you want to stand out online.

In a world full of AI content and faceless brands trust has become the most valuable currency on LinkedIn. And you are key. Your headshot is what gives the first impression. Warm, professional, approachable. Your profile tells people who you are, who you help and how.

But here's the opportunity right now. AI has created a gap. Real voices, real faces, real expertise. It's time to build trust. That's what people are hungry for right now. People buy from people they know, like and trust.

Be consistent with your message.

Show up consistently and lead with your knowledge (show you know your stuff). Do what you say you will do. Deliver results. (People talk. They will tell people about you).

> People buy from people.

How are you building trust on LinkedIn?

Trust is the new currency.

LinkedIn is where you can build trust ...and trust is what drives revenue.

SXSW has always been a place where the future shows up a little early; but this year, it feels like the future is arriving right now…messy, robotic, and very human at the same time. Coming back to Austin for the first time since my medical leave has been both energizing and disorienting in the best ways. A lot has changed. Not just for me personally, but across downtown Austin and the overall rhythm of the conference. Without the convention center as a grounding force, the city feels more spread out. The festival more diffuse. With Innovation, Music, and Film all running concurrently, the once-clear lines between industries have blurred into something more fluid and at times, more chaotic. There’s incredible cross-pollination, but fewer of those concentrated moments where your specific industry gathers and networking or bizdev happen naturally. More so than ever, SXSW reflects the broader media and tech landscape: Everything is converging at once. And when everything converges, patterns start to emerge. Here are a few shaping the Southby conversation: 1. AI has shifted from experimental to a necessary economic force. The shift this year is striking. The conversation has moved beyond “what can AI do?” to who owns it, who profits, and how value is distributed. For digital marketing, this raises big questions about content ownership, brand safety, and how we build sustainable models in an AI-driven ecosystem. 2. The creator economy is being rebuilt in real time. We’re seeing the rise of AI-powered production paired with creator-led distribution. The most forward-thinking brands aren’t just marketing anymore. They’re collaborating, co-creating, and enabling. It’s less about campaigns and more about continuous, algorithmically driven storytelling. 3. The rise of “human tech.” As everything becomes more automated, the premium on authenticity, trust, and emotional connection is only incre

# Microsoft Restructures Copilot Leadership Amid AI Rollout

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

3w

Five days. That’s how long it took between [Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text) unveiling [#Copilot](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fcopilot&trk=public_post-text) Health and Satya Nadella restructuring Copilot leadership. On March 12, Microsoft introduced Copilot Health, a phased U.S. rollout that lets adults connect medical records, wearables, lab data, and health history inside a dedicated health space. Microsoft framed it as part of a path toward “medical superintelligence.” On March 17, Nadella unified consumer and commercial Copilot under Jacob Andreou, formerly of Snap, while Mustafa Suleyman shifted his primary focus toward Microsoft’s superintelligence and model efforts. The official story is coherence and strategic focus. Maybe. But the backdrop matters. Microsoft says Copilot now has more than 150 million monthly active users across first-party platforms. Even so, Microsoft 365 Copilot has 15 million paid seats against a base of more than 450 million paid Microsoft 365 users. That is meaningful scale, but still modest paid penetration for the product at the center of Microsoft’s AI story. Why this matters for healthcare is simple. Microsoft is still the one Big Tech player with a meaningful shot at linking consumer health AI to real clinical workflow. Dragon Copilot already reaches more than 100,000 clinicians, and Copilot Health is clearly aimed at becoming the patient-facing layer. By contrast, OpenAI and Anthropic now both have healthcare offerings too, but Microsoft still appears to have the deepest current workflow footprint. The architecture is compelling. But architecture is not execution. And Microsoft just made clear that the person who evangelized the Copilot vision is no longer the primary owner of the product experience. The healthcare AI strategy still makes sense. The real question is whether Microsoft can actually ship it. We are pleased to announce a major milestone in an ongoing research initiative led by [Society for Inclusive and Collaborative Entrepreneurship (S4ICE)](https://de.linkedin.com/company/s4ice?trk=public_post-text), a registered German NGO headquartered in Stuttgart. Under the leadership of Dr. Jacques Yana Mbena (PhD, PLDA), Associate Professor of Management Sciences, Associate Editor of the Journal of Entrepreneurship in Emerging Economies, Corporate Treasurer, and Editor of this encyclopedia project, the Society launched in 2024 a global research initiative on Knowledge Management in Corporate Treasury. We launched this initiative to bridge the gap between academic research and practitioner insights in corporate treasury. We extend our sincere appreciation to the 120+ participants worldwide who have contributed quantitative data to support this effort. 📘 Project Milestone We are proud to share that the first full draft of the encyclopedia is now complete: Encyclopedia of Corporate Treasury in the Digital Era: Transforming the Function of Treasurer in the Time of Digital Platforms, AI, and Programmable Money As the project enters its next phase, we invite treasury professionals, academics, and industry experts to contribute to selected parts of the encyclopedia. ✍️ Call for Contributions Contributors are invited to express interest in specific Parts and Chapters (detailed sections and subsections are available in the attached document): - Part One: Managing Treasury in a Changing World - Part Two: Treasury Core in the Digital Era - Part Three: Treasury in the Times of AI and Programmable Money - Part Four: The Future of Treasury in a Diversified and Turbulent World For any interest in contributing, please contact [Jacques Yana Mbena, PhD](https://de.linkedin.com/in/jacques-yana-mbena-phd-24015536?trk=public_post-

The FTC Just Changed the Rules of the Game in Healthcare Antitrust It’s no longer just about class of trade, market share or pricing. The new question: Who controls the patient journey? In my latest Pharmaceutical Executive article, I break down how the FTC is shifting toward an ecosystem view of competition, especially in vertically integrated models like CVS Health. Lo storytelling non è una dote. È una strategia. 🎙️ Tutti pensiamo di saper comunicare. Ma c'è una differenza enorme tra trasmettere informazioni e raccontare qualcosa che resta. [Veronica Capozzi](https://it.linkedin.com/in/veronicacapozzi?trk=public_post-text) pone una questione che mi ha fatto riflettere: il nostro cervello non percepisce le storie come minacciose. Di fronte a una narrazione, abbassa le difese, attiva l'empatia e apre il pensiero creativo. Di fronte a dati e argomenti, invece, si mette in modalità valutazione — e spesso resistenza. Questo cambia tutto, in ambito professionale. Al colloquio di lavoro non vinci elencando competenze. Vinci raccontando chi sei e dove stai andando. Come manager o professionista non convinci con le slide. Convinci con le storie di chi ha già ottenuto risultati grazie a te. Nella comunicazione aziendale non basta pubblicare. Bisogna pianificare una narrazione coerente — sui social come nella relazione con clienti e collaboratori. Lo storytelling è tra le competenze più sottovalutate nel mondo HR e del business. Non perché sia difficile — ma perché in pochi la trattano con la stessa serietà di un'analisi di dati o di una strategia commerciale. La domanda che mi faccio oggi è questa: Nelle ultime due settimane, quante volte hai usato una storia per comunicare qualcosa di importante… e quante volte hai usato solo fatti?

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fdeborah-limb-6505066a_sarah-thanks-for-posting-i-am-reflecting-activity-7442922970014138369-egCc&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

Sarah, Thanks for posting! I am reflecting on the cultural impact at Boeing during Alan’s leadership. The working together system was a great new method to integrate the complex systems, functions, suppliers and stakeholders into one plan. Beyond just building an effective performing, integrated team, Alan showed us the way to engage our heart in our leadership through the required principles and practices expected of all leaders. The first time I saw the values posted on the wall, I was truly surprised. The working together values looked like this: *People first… Love them up. *Everyone is included compelling vision, comprehensive strategy, and relentless implementations. *Respect, listen, help, and appreciate each other. *Emotional resilience, trust the process, and each other…..and more! Alan showed us how to deliver while engaging, motivating, and respecting the people. I remember one meeting when he challenged the leaders to respectfully get off our phones and listen to the presenter! When he said, love them up, it didn’t mean the soft license to do whatever you want. It meant engagement, respect, personal accountability, and unifying under one plan with a compelling vision to get something amazing done!

- 

 

Iran has destroyed an AWACS aircraft. A missile strike hit Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia. Two Boeing KC-135 refueling aircraft also sustained significant damage. Prior to this, at a base near Baghdad, a UH-60 Black Hawk helicopter and an AN/MPQ-64 Sentinel multi-functional radar, designed to detect air targets at medium and long ranges, were attacked by an FPV drone. Two days ago, Hezbollah struck a Merkava tank in Lebanon with an FPV drone. War is changing. Completely. Of course, tanks, planes, and aircraft carriers aren’t going to disappear into the past; they continue to be important, but they’re becoming defenseless against cheap drones, and no countermeasure has been found yet. Instead of laughing at the Russian warship, the world—to which Ukraine has gifted an incredible four years—should have woken up to all these changes the day before yesterday and started developing countermeasures. But no. The AWACS are lined up at the airbase like in a parade. The Black Hawk stands in an open area. The radar too. The Merkava without its turret. As if those four years never happened. They were preparing for the last war all over again. The head of Rheinmetall is certainly right, and when giant military conglomerates really get into drone production—it will be a whole different level, a whole different scale, and a whole different kind of innovation. Without a doubt. The world—and, most importantly, war—will change in ways that will blow our minds. The Terminator is getting closer and closer to reality.

April 3, 1996. Dubrovnik, Croatia. A U.S. Air Force Boeing T-43A, carrying Commerce Secretary Ron Brown and a delegation of industry leaders, impacted terrain just miles from the runway. All 35 on board were lost. The accident was a controlled flight into terrain during a non-precision (NDB) approach—one that leaves little margin for error, especially in challenging conditions. Investigations found multiple contributing factors: procedural deviations, navigation inaccuracies, limited situational awareness, and environmental conditions, including reduced visibility and difficult terrain. The aircraft was not precisely aligned with the published approach course—placing it off centerline and into rising terrain. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐇𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧: 𝐍𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 This accident is often cited as a case study. Small shortcuts. Minor deviations. Things that “worked” before. Over time, they stop feeling like risks—and start feeling normal. The IFOR-21 crew may have experienced similar deviations without consequence. Until the environment stopped being forgiving. The question for any operation—aviation or otherwise: Are your teams truly following the process… Or are they just getting away with it? Because when the approach becomes unstable— when something doesn’t feel right— The most important safeguard isn’t experience alone. It’s the discipline to say: “We’re unstable. Going around.” So ask yourself: Does your culture actively support conservative decisions, such as a go-around? Or does it quietly expect the landing—no matter what? In aviation, it’s rarely one big failure. It’s the small ones that align.

"AI Drug Discovery" often sounds like a corporate buzzword for making pharma more efficient—faster, cheaper, and more automated. But when viewed through the lens of the CancerX Accelerator (and participated CancerX AstraZeneca assembly on 02Mar) , the mission becomes much more human: providing digital solutions that actually improve a patient's quality of life. Cancer patients don't just fight a disease; they fight financial toxicity, the loss of their daily lifestyle, and immense emotional distress. In this light, AI’s role shifts from merely 'making molecules' to 'saving years of a human life.' By making virtual cells and lessening the failure rate, we aren't just helping pharma companies; we wish to shorten the agonizing wait for a right cure and reducing the trial-and-error period that exhausts a patient’s body and spirit. Prendre la tête d’AstraZeneca France est à la fois un honneur et une responsabilité majeure, que j’accueille avec humilité et détermination. C’est une étape qui s’inscrit dans le prolongement de près de quinze années d’engagement au service de l’innovation thérapeutique et de son accès, auprès des équipes, des soignants, des associations et des patients. Chaque projet, chaque débat, chaque rencontre m’a préparée à cette responsabilité et je mesure pleinement ma chance d'avoir été aussi bien entourée. Je veux d’abord remercier [Anne-Laure Dreno](https://fr.linkedin.com/in/anne-laure-dreno?trk=public_post-text) pour son leadership et l’élan qu’elle a donné à la filiale ces dernières années. Son exigence, sa vision et son sens du collectif ont marqué nos équipes et nos résultats. Je lui souhaite beaucoup de succès dans ses nouvelles fonctions en tant que Senior Vice President, Global Respiratory & Immunology. Merci également aux collègues, mentors, partenaires et leaders avec qui j’ai eu la chance de travailler en France et à Cambridge — en oncologie, en hématologie et au-delà. À mes équipes Oncologie-Hématologie en France, un immense merci. Votre engagement, votre rigueur scientifique et votre proximité avec les problématiques de chaque parcours de soin ont été la force motrice de nos avancées, et une source infinie pour moi de motivation et d’énergie. J’ai une confiance totale dans votre capacité à continuer de questionner, d’innover et de relever les prochains défis. Avec un pipeline parmi les plus riches de l’industrie et l’ambition de lancer 20 nouveaux médicaments d’ici 2030 dans toutes nos aires thérapeutiques, nous avons au niveau de notre filiale une responsabilité particulière : transformer la science en bénéfices concrets, plus tôt et pour plus de patients. Cela suppose d’améliorer l’accès aux innovations mais aussi de créer les conditions d’un déploiement plus rapide de la recherche clinique sur notre territoire. Ce sont mes priorités et je mettrai toute mon énergie pour que la France reste un pays où chacun peut bénéficier des meilleurs progrès thérapeutiques. HSBC is considering a significant round of job cuts that could affect up to 20,000 roles over the coming years as it accelerates an AI-led transformation, Bloomberg reported, according to media reports. The move is expected to focus on reducing headcount in middle- and back-office functions as the bank integrates AI across operations. According to the report, non-client-facing roles within global services are likely to be the most affected. However, the review remains at an early stage, and no final decisions have been made. Some roles may not be replaced with AI, the report said, while a portion of workforce reductions could also result from potential business sales or exits. If implemented, the cuts could impact about 10% of HSBC’s global workforce, highlighting how AI adoption may reshape traditional banking operations. A 10-second reveal, backed by a week of preparation. For HSBC Hong Kong’s 150th anniversary, the unveiling itself lasted only 10 seconds but the preparation behind it took an entire week. Our task was to fully conceal the sculpture with a 9-meter-tall inflatable structure, then create a reveal moment that felt bold, striking, and unforgettable. What makes projects like this so rewarding is that the audience only sees a brief moment. What we see is everything behind it: planning, engineering, coordination, testing, and the collective effort it takes to make that moment happen. Sometimes the shortest moments demand the greatest precision. The only limitation is your imagination. Project in collaboration with Jack Morton. Mountain and Sea Scotland: Solway sojourn - sayonara in the sunshine

The day warmed up considerably as we crossed from Murray's Isles towards Ravenshall Point, so we took the opportunity to land and [change out of our drysuits on a tiny (and fast disappearing) beach](http://seakayakphoto.blogspot.co.uk/2015/11/a-race-against-tide-at-corbies-cove.html) at Corbie's (Crow's) Cove. Although it was mid October the weather was warmer and more settled than it had been for much of the cold and windy summer of 2015. Douglas had mentioned an interesting rock formation at [Ravenshall Point](http://www.streetmap.co.uk/map.srf?x=252500&y=552500&z=120&sv=ravenshall+point&st=3&tl=Map+of+Ravenshall+Point,+Dumfries+and+Galloway&searchp=ids.srf&mapp=map.srf), so I was curious to see what might be around the corner....

The rock formation here is really striking, a graceful sweep of pale rock forms an arch as it joins a larger sweeping outcrop with a window right through to the other side of the headland. Remarkably this great piece of rock architecture is less than a hundred metres from the busy A75 trunk road which runs along the line of a raised beach above the present day shore, yet it could be a million miles away. We enjoyed reversing in under the arch to get a better view before reluctantly.....

...turning our boats and starting the last leg of our Solway sojourn. There was plenty of exploration left to do though, the coast between Ravenshall Point and the end of our journey is punctuated by a couple of large caravan sites but also has great stretches of rockhopping.....

...with more amazing rock formations. The rock of this crag was so contorted that it was difficult to tell what the predominant plane of it might be. Amongst the paler bands were sections of bright rose pink rock picked out by the sunshine - how many of the drivers speeding by on the road above could imagine what's just below them?

At the back of a sun-warmed cove we saw three lovely [Red Admiral](http://butterfly-conservation.org/679-823/red-admiral-.html) butterflies (Vanessa atalanta) which seemed to be actively seeking out the splashes of water on the rocks. They were probably feeding on the flowers of the Ivy which covers most of the cliffs here. These butterflies are unlikely to survive the Scottish winter, the species is mainly migratory in the UK with the population topped up by an influx of butterflies from the continent each Spring.

We intended to land near Cardoness at about high water to avoid a long portage with the boats so we had some time in hand to explore the intricate rockhopping opportunities. Douglas is back there somewhere......

...and showing fine style through a narrow channel :o)

As the cliffs begin to peter out there are a series of dry coves cutting back into the level of the raised beach. This one had pine trees perfectly placed at either end and looked almost like a Japanese coastal scene - perhaps a fitting backdrop as we said Sayonara to our Solway journ

Holywell Birding: solway

The past couple of days have been spent with Gary Storey, starting in Cumbria on Thursday. Heading over early our first destination was the Solway, in search of the reported American Golden Plover, unfortunately we never did find the it but we were treated to an assortment of other waders compromising of Redshank, Lapwing, Curlew, Bar-Tailed Godwit, Dunlin, Knot, Golden Plover, Snipe and Ruff, with a supporting cast of 4 Scaup, 1 Great Crested Grebe, 3 Pintail, Barnacle Geese, Pink-Footed Geese, and a massive amount of Lesser Black Backed Gulls.

The Solway itself was unbelievably calm, almost mirror like, the vastness of this landscape is always memorising.

Next stop was Martindale in the hope of some early rutting, this wasn't to be the case but we did find over 200+ Red Deer and a few young stags. The weather continued to be good to us and so we spent a good few hours in the valley. I'll be revisiting the area in a couple of weeks time.

Our final spot of the day was the Badger Sett, I've visited this site a number of times and without fail I've been rewarded. Meeting [Andrew](http://andrew-jake.blogspot.co.uk/) at 18:30 we quietly made our way to the viewpoint, within ten minutes or so your absorb into the landscape, senses finely tuned. Generally its an hour - an hour and a half wait for the Badgers so your attention turns to the wildlife going about its business as the sun sets. The first of which was the hammering of the Great Spotted Woodpecker, unseen but definitely heard, the visualised image of it foraging for grubs in the branches dominated the mind. Next a Robin began feeding beneath us, a birds eye view of its activity, completely oblivious to our presence and a nearby Blackbird bathed in a small pool, yet again unseen but every splash could be seen, in the mind.

The use of all the senses whilst watching wildlife has always interested me, experience the same event in a number of ways. Taking away one of those senses brings an entirely different sense of feeling to that particular moment. I like to think it takes you back to the bare bones, no over thinking, just the realisation and appreciation of the moment.

As the last of the light faded the Blackbirds began alarm calling in the plantation to our right, the reaction to an emerging Tawny Owl, within a couple of minutes it began to echo out its own call.

The Badgers soon began to emerge, the first individual gave great views as it sniffed around the sett, scratching as it went, at one point laying down to scratch its front. One Badger soon turned into 7, once they had made their way away from the sett to feed, we quietly snook out from out viewpoint and back to the car.

Friday started a bit later with the plan to head for Holy Island, unfortunately I mixed the tides up and we missed our crossing, so instead we headed for Seahouses for some fish and chips and to meet some of the locals.

A trip to Seahouses isn't complete without feeding the Eiders.

Iain Dale's Diary: Is Twitter for Tw*ts?

Twitter is a form of social media which some people in the political and media world really do seem to have an issue with. [Rachel Sylvester](http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/comment/columnists/rachel_sylvester/article5877318.ece) in this morning's Times launches a tirade against people who use Twitter, relying on clapped out psychologist Oliver James to make her point for her...

> “Twittering stems from a lack of identity. It's a constant update of who you are, what you are, where you are. Nobody would Twitter if they had a strong sense of identity.”

Gee thanks for that insight. What a load of old cobblers. Bit it fits Sylvester's narrative that everyone who twitters must have something missing in their lives. She may be right about some, but it's also what MSM journalists used to say about blogging, which many of them have now either chosen to or been forced to take up. Rachel also says that "Twitter is reality TV without the pictures". I think there is a small amount of truth in that, especially when you look at those who twitter obsessively, every minute of the day. I am thinking of some of the celebrity twitterers like Stephen Fry or Philip Schofield whose every movement seems to be catalogued in too much detail. It was for that reason that I was a comparatively late developer on Twitter.But having decided how I wanted to use it, and that I wouldn't do it too often (So far this month it has ranged between twice and seven times a day) I have grown to rather enjoy it. For me, it complements the blog brand and strengthens the feeling of community I have tried to engender within the blog. I have not gone out to tart myself around Twitter (more of this in a moment) but I seem to be getting 40 or 50 new followers each day. I now have more than 2,500, which is around a fifth of the number of people who read the blog each day. I have around 150 people whose feeds I follow on Twitter, but that number has grown too far and I am about to cut it back. Stan Collymore is about to be dumped into the Twatterbox, for instance. And not before time! He, like Jonathan Ross, suffers from Twittahhorrea. [Guido](http://www.order-order.com/2009/03/case-against-twitter.html) has written a very thoughtful post on the benefits of Twitter today too. He uses Twitter purely as a broadcast mechanism in order to drive more readers to his blog. His only ever tweets to advertise a new blogpost. He says...

> Iain Dale positively relishes keeping us up to date with his movements - why anyone besides Mrs Fawkes would be interested in knowing that Guido was stuffing porridge into a recalcitrant 2 year old at 7.30 a.m. or Guinness into himself at 7.30 p.m. is bewildering.

I think that is to fundamentally misunderstand the medium. I don't recall ever using Twitter in such a manner (unless, as Esther Rantzen might say, you know different). Some people do use it to describe their everyday lives, as if we should be interested in what time

In 2000, BP hired Ogilvy & Mather. In 2004, that partnership gave the world the carbon footprint calculator. Not out of concern for the planet. To redirect the conversation. The idea was simple: - Make climate change a personal responsibility. - Give individuals a tool to measure their guilt. - Run the campaign globally. It worked. For 20 years, we calculated our flights, our diets, our commutes. Meanwhile, just 100 fossil fuel companies like BP, Shell, and Chevron were responsible for 71% of all global industrial emissions since 1988. They didn't just pollute. They designed the metric that made it your problem. Your carbon footprint is a PR strategy. And it's a very good one.

# New York Proposes Taxing Startup Gains, Threatening Entrepreneurship

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

 

 

[](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rachel-ten-brink-a08151?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Rachel ten Brink](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rachel-ten-brink-a08151?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) 

 1w Edited 

 

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Frachel-ten-brink-a08151_new-york-is-proposing-decoupling-from-the-activity-7441243661096419328-JaNN&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

New York is proposing decoupling from the federal QSBS exclusion, which would effectively tax startup founders, early employees and investors on gains that the federal government specifically designed to be tax-free to incentivize entrepreneurship. As a founder turned VC, I've lived the years of sacrifice it takes to build a company from nothing to an exit, on both sides of the table. QSBS exists because we as a country decided that risk-taking and company building should be rewarded. New York is now saying: we'd like the upside, thanks. (and the kicker- they want to make it retroactive to Jan 2025). Here's what Albany doesn't seem to understand: Early-stage founders are the most mobile people in the economy. No 500-person team to relocate. No office lease to break. Just a laptop and a dream. When QSBS matters most, at founding and early growth, there is almost zero friction to leaving. And they will. This won't generate the revenue they're projecting. It will push founders to Miami, Austin, Nashville and every other city that's been rolling out the welcome mat. It will make LPs think twice. I've been here as New York spent years building one of the best startup ecosystems in the country… and this is so deeply flawed.

 

-

# Unilever reportedly sells food business to McCormick for €29 billion

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

 

 

[](https://ae.linkedin.com/in/peternorwell?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Peter Norwell](https://ae.linkedin.com/in/peternorwell?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) 

 5d 

 

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fpeternorwell_another-major-one-potentially-on-the-table-activity-7442919553308876801-Uizq&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

Another major one potentially on the table. [Unilever](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/unilever?trk=public_post-text) is reportedly in discussions to sell its food business to [McCormick & Company](https://www.linkedin.com/company/mccormick?trk=public_post-text) in a deal that could be worth as much as €29 billion. If it happens, brands like Hellmann’s, Knorr, Marmite, and Colman’s could all move into new hands. What stands out to me is that this is far bigger than a typical acquisition. It feels like a real strategic reset on both sides. Unilever continues to sharpen its focus around beauty and personal care, while McCormick would be making a huge statement about where it wants to play globally and how quickly it wants to scale. This is also not a new approach for McCormick. Some of its best known brands have been acquired rather than built organically, and that tells you a lot about how the biggest players are choosing to grow. If this deal goes through, it would be a landmark move and could significantly reshape the global condiments and flavourings category. The broader question for me is still the same though. If so much of the growth at the top end is being driven by acquisition, who is really building the next generation of breakout food brands from the ground up? It does make you wonder whether organic brand building is slowly becoming the exception rather than the norm.

 

-

Liquid I.V. commits 1 percent of its annual revenue to water restoration, quality and access projects. In 2025, that translated into an investment of $4.29 million in projects to expand access to clean drinking water for more than 1 million people. The Unilever brand's goal is to improve access for more than 2 million people by the end of 2026. I've linked to the company's new impact report, published today, so you can read about the projects and organizations that it works with. This is the first phase of the company's sustainability plan: it is developing other goals that it plans to publish this year. [Jayce Newton](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jaycenewton?trk=public_post-text), the company's director of impact, told me that it focused first on restoration for many reasons, including this one: people need drinkable freshwater to consumer Liquid I.V.'s supplements. So, in a sense, that 1 percent number is probably well-justified in the company's marketing budget. I find it notable, though, that [Unilever](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/unilever?trk=public_post-text) chose to let this practice continue after it acquired the company in September 2020. This is the most successful brand in its health and wellness group. It has also encouraged Liquid I.V. to publish its best practices for selecting nonprofit water partners that can deliver on projects. (Also outlined in the linked report.) Most sustainability efforts act like a skin, a superficial layer of "green" messaging applied at the end. True circularity behaves like an operating system: it is the fundamental logic that governs your supply chain, your R&D, and your quality control. At Banyan Nation, we’ve built the OS for Better Plastic™: - The Logic: 6-stage scientific protocols that ensure resin behaves like virgin material. - The Security: 100% digital traceability for audit-ready compliance. - The Output: Brand-approved quality that doesn't fail on high-speed manufacturing lines. Stop treating the planet as a slogan. Start treating it as a system. # Lucy Georgiades

Founder & CEO at Elevate Leadership

## About

I've dedicated my career to helping leaders overcome a myriad of challenges. My journey has taken me from London to Silicon Valley, where I've spent over a decade coaching Founders, CEOs, executive teams, and leaders at all levels. I've spent thousands of hours coaching and training leaders, helping them navigate their challenges, communicate effectively, achieve their goals, and lead their teams. I've worked closely with founders and leaders of all levels to enhance their communication skills, and scale themselves and their teams. I've also supported leaders in their personal development, helping them understand their strengths, identify areas for growth, and get to the next level. Change is a constant in any organization, and I've guided leaders through these periods of change. I've helped them find the positives, anticipate questions, get their teams on board, and proactively monitor their teams' reactions. My goal is to empower leaders to navigate these challenges with confidence and grace. My passion lies in designing, writing, and running management workshops that are not only memorable so people implement what they’ve learned, but also enjoyable. The programs I design are rooted in science, highly effective and actionable. Over the last 10 years, I've designed large-scale, multi-year management development training programs for companies of various sizes across the US. My academic background is in cognitive neuropharmacology and vision and brain development, fields that delve deep into understanding the relationships between the brain and human behavior. This knowledge, combined with my extensive coaching experience, has given me a unique insight into what truly works when it comes to leadership. I find immense joy in creating relevant, actionable content that equips as many people as possible with the confidence and tools to lead effectively. When I'm not coaching or designing workshops, I love to swim and paint canvases for my family and friends. It's these passions that fuel my work and drive my desire to create positive change in the world of leadership.

- 

 

Autonomous haulage in mining is moving from pilot projects to serious operational consideration, and 2026 is shaping up to be a year where more sites will have to decide if they are ready or not. The technology itself, lidar, radar, GNSS, and AI working together, is mature enough. What is less straightforward is everything around it: workforce transitions, network infrastructure, and cybersecurity protocols that most mine sites are still building out. One thing worth noting is that a lot of the adoption numbers circulating right now come from a single commercial source, so independent validation is still needed before treating them as benchmarks. The real question for operators is not whether autonomous haulage works. It is whether the surrounding systems and people are ready to support it at scale. - [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Favantgarde-int_yimin-coal-mine-what-100-ai-driven-electric-activity-7439627489993183232-wzNL&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

🤖 100 autonomous electric trucks in a coal mine: mining innovation… or an energy paradox?🤖 At the Yimin coal mine in Inner Mongolia, a fleet of 100 AI-driven autonomous electric trucks connected via 5G is already operating in extreme conditions of up to -48°C. The project represents one of the mining sector’s largest real-world technology experiments. ⚙️ It combines three key pillars of the mine of the future: • Electrification, with high-capacity batteries and automated five-minute battery-swap stations. • AI-driven autonomy, removing drivers from hazardous environments. • Advanced connectivity (5G-A) enabling entire fleets to operate in real time. ⛏️ The result: greater safety, reduced human exposure and potentially higher productivity. But it also raises a deeper question. 🌍 If technology makes coal extraction safer, more efficient and more economical, are we accelerating the energy transition… or extending the commercial life of coal? For the mining industry, projects like this show that digitalisation is no longer experimental, it’s strategic. 💬 Do you think electrification and automation in coal mining truly support decarbonisation, or could they ultimately reinforce the sector’s longevity? # BP's Carbon Footprint Calculator: A PR Strategy to Shift Blame

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

 

 

[](https://in.linkedin.com/in/harshalkalambe?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Harshal Kalambe](https://in.linkedin.com/in/harshalkalambe?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) 

 2w 

 

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fharshalkalambe_in-2000-bp-hired-ogilvy-mather-in-2004-activity-7439890776970592256--H4D&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

In 2000, BP hired Ogilvy & Mather. In 2004, that partnership gave the world the carbon footprint calculator. Not out of concern for the planet. To redirect the conversation. The idea was simple: - Make climate change a personal responsibility. - Give individuals a tool to measure their guilt. - Run the campaign globally. It worked. For 20 years, we calculated our flights, our diets, our commutes. Meanwhile, just 100 fossil fuel companies like BP, Shell, and Chevron were responsible for 71% of all global industrial emissions since 1988. They didn't just pollute. They designed the metric that made it your problem. Your carbon footprint is a PR strategy. And it's a very good one.

# Pfizer, Merck, Gilead: Why Big Pharma Loves Cancer

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

[](https://www.linkedin.com/in/paullobosco?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

[Paul Lobosco](https://www.linkedin.com/in/paullobosco?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)

5d Edited

- [Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Fpaullobosco_prevention-or-treatment-why-big-pharma-is-activity-7439695778459152384-9sUE&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting)

Prevention or Treatment? Why Big pHARMa is in business today. Prevention- removing a carcinogen from the water supply or banning a toxic food additive costs a company money and generates $0 in medical revenue. Treatment- A single patient can represent $500,000+ in revenue over several years. Ahh, jackpot. In the case of Pfizer, following the decline of COVID-19 vaccine sales, Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla pivoted the entire company toward cancer. He recently described the company's $43 billion acquisition of Seagen as a "bold move to tackle critical areas of unmet need." Analysts note that Pfizer is moving away from primary care (keeping people healthy) and toward "specialty oncology." Why? Because oncology drugs have some of the highest profit margins in the world and are shielded from the "patent cliffs" that kill off cheaper medications. In the case of Merck, CEO Rob Davis on a recent call highlighted that the company has line of sight to $70 billion in commercial opportunity by the mid-2030s. He stated, "Today, our business is anchored by an important set of commercial products that address critical unmet needs... representing the majority of our $70 billion of non-risk-adjusted commercial opportunity." Translation: Merck is explicitly counting on a steady stream of sick people to double their revenue from their current blockbuster, Keytruda. For Gilead, the biggest mistake they ever made was to make a drug that cured Hep C. Their sales subsequently plummeted. They'll never make that mistake again. And it goes on. [Callio Therapeutics](https://www.linkedin.com/company/calliotx?trk=public_post-text) Advances ADC Innovation: First Patient Dosed in Phase I Trial of CLIO-8221 The future of cancer treatment is evolving; and Callio Therapeutics is taking a bold step forward. The company has successfully dosed the first patient in its Phase I clinical trial of CLIO-8221, a first-in-class dual-payload antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting HER2-expressing solid tumors. What makes CLIO-8221 different? Combines two powerful mechanisms: Topo1 inhibitor + ATR inhibitor Designed to overcome resistance seen in traditional single-payload ADCs Shows strong tumor regression even in resistant models (preclinical data) Built with a next-gen linker platform to reduce toxicity & improve safety Trial Expansion & Regulatory Milestones Ongoing trials across Australia & the U.S. IND clearance received from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration Expansion planned in China (under review by National Medical Products Administration) Leadership Perspective As CEO Piers Ingram highlights, this milestone reflects their commitment to advancing multi-mechanism targeted therapies for cancer. CSO [Jerome Boyd-Kirkup](https://sg.linkedin.com/in/jeromeboydkirkup?trk=public_post-text) emphasized the superior anti-tumor activity and potential to outperform single-payload ADCs. CMO [Naomi Hunder](https://www.linkedin.com/in/naomi-hunder-b420525?trk=public_post-text) noted that CLIO-8221 could offer new hope for patients resistant to existing HER2 therapies, with improved safety profiles. # Diageo's Manipulative Alcohol Education

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

[Lucy Rocca](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/lucy-rocca-871a458b?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name) 

 1w 

 

- [ Report this post](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fposts%2Flucy-rocca-871a458b_alcoholawareness-publichealth-sobriety-activity-7439653034390896640-YnWU&trk=public_post_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=POST&_f=guest-reporting) 
- 

 

This isn’t education. It’s narrative control. I read a piece this week celebrating Diageo’s Smashed Live being delivered to thousands of schoolchildren. It’s framed as “vital education.” But let’s be clear what we’re dealing with. This is a multi-billion-pound alcohol company - whose profits depend on people drinking - positioning itself as a trusted voice in how young people understand alcohol. Pause on that. If the intention was genuinely to reduce drinking… 👉 what happens to future customers? 👉 what happens to shareholder expectations? You cannot reduce demand and grow profits at the same time. So something has to give. Look at the messaging. It’s always: make better choices understand peer pressure drink responsibly But never: - alcohol is a Group 1 carcinogen - there is no completely safe level of consumption - not drinking is a valid, positive option Sobriety is missing. Controlled drinking is centred. That’s not accidental. And here’s the clever bit. The focus is always on: 👉 the individual 👉 behaviour 👉 choices So the problem becomes you. Not: 👉 the product 👉 the marketing 👉 the environments designed to make drinking feel normal Ask yourself: Why is cancer barely mentioned? Why is sobriety never presented as a lifestyle choice? Why are alcohol companies anywhere near schools in the first place? This isn’t neutral. This is a multi-billion-pound industry shaping the narrative early - before drinking even begins. Because if you can embed the idea that alcohol is simply part of life - something to “handle responsibly” - you never have to defend it later. Wake up. This is manipulation in plain sight. # Sharon’s Policy at Five: A Domestic Abuse Workplace Policy Supporting UK Employers

- [Contact](https://domestic-abuse.co.uk/contact/)
- [Domestic Abuse Alliance](https://domestic-abuse.co.uk/)

- Sharon’s Policy at Five: A Domestic Abuse Workplace Policy Supporting UK Employers

As we mark five years of Sharon’s Policy, we are reminded of something we see every day in our work with survivors: domestic abuse does not stay at home; it follows people into the workplace.

At Domestic Abuse Alliance, we work closely with frontline services, legal partners and employers across the UK. We understand how critical the workplace can be – often the only safe space where someone experiencing abuse may feel able to seek help.

### A Changing National Landscape

In December 2025, the UK Government published its [Violence Against Women and Girls (VAWG) Strategy and Action Plan](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/freedom-from-violence-and-abuse-a-cross-government-strategy), setting out strengthened expectations for employers.

For the first time, employers are being clearly recognised as key actors in prevention, early intervention and reporting.

This shift reflects a growing understanding that domestic abuse is not only a personal issue – it is also a workplace safeguarding issue.

Employers are now expected to:

- Maintain clear domestic abuse workplace policies
- Provide safe and confidential reporting pathways
- Strengthen safeguarding and support measures
- Engage with national networks such as EIDA
- Prepare for increased monitoring and accountability

This represents a significant step forward but also a clear challenge: many organisations are still not equipped to respond confidently to domestic abuse disclosures.

### Why Sharon’s Policy Matters

Long before these national expectations were introduced, [Sharon’s Policy](https://domesticabuseeducation.co.uk/sharons-domestic-abuse-policy/) has given thousands of organisations the tools to respond to disclosures of abuse confidently and consistently.

Launched in 2021, Sharon’s Policy was co-created by [Sharon Livermore MBE](https://domesticabuseeducation.co.uk/), [the Employers’ Initiative on Domestic Abuse (EIDA)](https://www.eida.org.uk/), [The HR Dept](https://www.hrdept.co.uk/), and [Domestic Abuse Alliance](https://domestic-abuse.co.uk/).

Developed as a free-to-use domestic abuse policy and guidance framework, it was designed to help employers recognise the signs of abuse, respond safely and support employees to access emotional and practical support to help them cope and recover.

Since then, it has become a trusted resource for organisations seeking to build safer, more informed workplace responses to domestic abuse.

More than 3,000 employers nationwide have now adopted Sharon’s Policy, w

Mourners gathered at a funeral in Londonderry on Thursday to remember Amy Doherty, a 28-year-old mother and care worker who died after being found badly injured on Saturday. The service at Holy Family Church heard tributes describing her "magnetic personality" and "joyful smile" as police continue to investigate her death.

Parish priest Fr Joe Gormley told mourners it was "a very sad day for the family of Amy Doherty, a very sad day for our parish and city." He described how "the death of Amy brought a darkness, a sadness, anguish deeper than any lack of daylight."

Fr Gormley reflected on Doherty's character, saying: "The darkness is, in a sense, deeply intensified when we hear the human story of the beauty of who Amy Doherty is. Amy is a person who has a magnetic personality who would draw people to her by her joyful smile."

# Mourners at Amy Doherty’s funeral hear of her ‘magnetic personality’

 

 

 

By PA News Agency

 

 

Share

[0 Comments](https://www.wandsworthguardian.co.uk/comments/25971510/)

 

Family and friends watch as the coffin is carried in to Holy Family Church (Mark Marlow/PA)

 

 

This article is brought to you by our exclusive subscriber partnership with our sister title USA Today, and has been written by our American colleagues. It does not necessarily reflect the view of The Herald.

Mourners at the funeral of Amy Doherty have been told she had a “magnetic personality” and “would draw people to her by her joyful smile”.

The 28-year-old mother was found badly injured by police on Saturday and died later in hospital.

A 30-year-old man has been arrested on suspicion of murder.

The funeral mass for Ms Doherty took place on Thursday at Holy Family Church in Ballymagroarty, Londonderry.

 Amy Doherty (PSNI/PA) 

Parish priest Fr Joe Gormley told the congregation it was “a very sad day for the family of Amy Doherty, a very sad day for our parish and city”.

He said mourners would be thinking of Rhea and Ronan, Ms Doherty’s two children, and would be trying to “make sense of the events of last Saturday”.

He said March 21 is a day where, for the first time in six months, “the time of light in our days exceeds the time of darkness”, but “the death of Amy brought a darkness, a sadness, anguish deeper than any lack of daylight”.

He said: “The darkness is, in a sense, deeply intensified when we hear the human story of the beauty of who Amy Doherty is.

“Amy is a person who has a magnetic personality who would draw people to her by her joyful smile.”

 A horse-drawn hearse leads the funeral cortege (Mark Marlow/PA) 

He described her as a “dedicated care worker” who lifted the spirits of patients at Altnagelvin Hospital, in Derry, during the pandemic.

“She’d always try and find fun, find humour, particularly in those dark days when people were very afraid,” he said.

He said she helped people set up video calls and encouraged patients to connect with people outside and enabled “those who maybe hadn’t connected with others for a long time, to begin to do so, and to use the opportunity of Covid to do that”.

However, he said, the “heart of her care” was her two children, who “are the centre of our thoughts and prayers today and beyond”.

Ms Doherty is survived by her parents, Patrick and Sharon, and her younger brothers, Shane and Ryan.

 Police at the scene in the Summer Meadows Mews area of Londonderry (Brian Lawless/PA) 

On Wednesday the PSNI said the man who was arrested was receiving medical treatment and was not fit for police interview.

According to Women’s Aid, Ms Doherty was the 30th woman in Northern Ireland to have been killed since 2020.

I'm now at the stage of tweeting fairly regularly (for me that is still only 2-3 times a week) which of course pales into insignificance compared to some Tweeters!I tweet from my own account (@SharonSchindler) and also on occasion from my work account (@csp_otago). And I'm feeling part of an online community.Just when I thought I was getting to grips with it, they launched a new version with more stuff for me to grasp - once again it is about lifelong learning and adapting to new ways.I find it great as a quick and easy way to follow headlines and hot topics, but not so great if those you want to hear about are not using it.I've more or less mastered using lists on there which I find great for following specific groups of people/businesses. The same applies to hashtags for easy real time results and up to date news for specific interest areas.I did join in the FO2010 Twitter class session, but found it a challenge to follow who was talking to who and if anyone was actually answering the questions posed, it was a little hectic and hard to follow and probably not a platform I would consider using at this stage for online meetings.I think I've now added the Twitter widget to my blog post!

Thousands of people have gathered in Derry for a vigil in memory of mother-of-two Amy Doherty.

People gathered at the Guildhall on Friday evening carrying flowers and purple balloons, hours after a man appeared in court charged with Doherty’s murder.

Women stood at the steps of the Guildhall, behind a line of candles and flowers, carrying photos of female victims of violence.

Amy’s mother Sharon, said, during an emotional address to the crowd, that her daughter had been “brutally taken from us and her babies”.

At her funeral on Thursday, Doherty was described as a “dedicated” care worker and a person with a “magnetic personality” who drew people to her.

Her death and other high-profile cases have prompted discussions on women’s and girls’ safety in Northern Ireland.

There was no application for bail and he was remanded in custody until April 23.

The saying assumption is the mother of all f**k ups could not be more true when applying it to business. Often it’s not that you are wrong it more you failed to understand what the real problem was. We spent years we building better training. Better content. Better structure. Better delivery. And it worked to a point. But it never quite solved the real issue. Because the real question isn’t How do we train our team better HOW DO WE SCALE WHAT ACTUALLY MAKES OUR TOP PERFORMERS EXCEPTIONAL Every founder says they want best practice. But what they really want is their way of winning, scaled. That’s where I went wrong. I assumed the market wanted an out of the box answer. It doesn’t. It wants a way to turn its own playbook into something repeatable. That’s where our Call Coach became the centre of everything. And importantly, every version of ORCA is built around that. All white labelled. All driven by Call Coach. Just applied in different ways depending on where you’re starting. 1. Call Coach over your existing LMS You’ve already invested in training. The issue isn’t content. It’s that behaviour isn’t changing. We plug Call Coach into your world, built around your roles, your messaging, your scenarios. So your team can practise, get feedback, and improve in your way of selling. 2. Ready to go ORCA with Call Coach, live coaching and fundamentals You need structure, quickly. No consistent training. Managers repeating themselves. Different standards across the team. We give you a full system, but still tailored to you. Your branding. Your focus areas. Your scenarios inside Call Coach. So from day one, it feels like your academy, not ours. 3. Build your own White labelled ORCA with Call Coach You’ve got top performers. But what they do lives in their heads. This is where we go deeper. We capture your playbook, your messaging, your BD approach

✨ Looking forward to attending the Women in Data Flagship Event ✨ I’m really excited to be joining the [Women in Data®](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/womenindata?trk=public_post-text) community at their upcoming Flagship Event, a space that consistently brings together insight, ambition and action across data, AI and analytics. Why this matters 👇 Despite progress, women still represent less than a third of the global AI workforce, and around one quarter of data & analytics roles. At the same time, women are disproportionately impacted by automation, yet underrepresented in shaping the systems being built. That imbalance isn’t just an equity issue; it’s a quality, trust and outcomes issue for AI itself. What I’m hoping to take away from the day: 🔹 Practical ideas for accelerating women’s progression into senior data & AI leadership 🔹 Honest conversations about what actually works when building inclusive, high‑performing data cultures 🔹 Fresh perspectives on how we design, deploy and govern AI responsibly, with diverse voices at the table 🔹 New connections with people who are as passionate about impact as they are about innovation Events like this matter because representation shapes reality, the data we collect, the models we train, the decisions we automate, and ultimately the outcomes we create for customers and society. If you’re attending, I’d love to connect. And if you’re not, follow along, because these conversations are ones our industry can’t afford not to have. 💬 What’s one thing you think would most accelerate women’s impact in data & AI right now? How is your workplace rolling out AI? Our latest report suggests room for improvement: [https://lnkd.in/eYJGbbK6](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeYJGbbK6&urlhash=_MR6&trk=public_post-text) Research from our AI Company Data Initiative in partnership with [UNESCO](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/unesco?trk=public_post-text), finds a gap between businesses’ ambition to harness the potential of AI, and the mechanisms to manage its risks – with significant impact for employees. 🧠 The most important AI question in legal tech isn't "which model?", it's "whose data?" [Thomson Reuters](https://ca.linkedin.com/company/thomson-reuters?trk=public_post-text) is launching [#Thomson](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fthomson&trk=public_post-text) this summer, their own legally-trained LLM, built on open-source foundations and pre-trained on terabytes of TR's proprietary legal, tax, and news content. Early benchmarks already show it outperforming general models on legal tasks. As our CTO [Joel Hron](https://www.linkedin.com/in/joel-hron-90a3421a?trk=public_post-text) describes it, Thomson is built to go wide across general language, then go deep where it matters most. For CoCounsel users, that means better outputs, often without even knowing Thomson is running underneath. The architecture is deliberately "T-shaped", broad general language capability with a deep vertical column of domain expertise. There's something worth sitting with here. TR isn't racing to build the biggest model, they're asking a quieter, harder question: what does a model actually need to know to be useful to a tax attorney at 11pm before a filing deadline? That specificity of purpose is what separates a product from a feature, and a workflow tool from something professionals genuinely rely on. 🔗 [Richard Tromans](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/artificiallawyer?trk=public_post-text) at [Artificial Lawyer](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/artificial-lawyer?trk=public_post-text) sat down with Joel to tell the full story, well worth your time. ✈️ [Boeing](https://www.linkedin.com/company/boeing?trk=public_post-text) 777X vs. [Airbus](https://fr.linkedin.com/company/airbusgroup?trk=public_post-text) A350‑1000 - The Battle for Tomorrow’s Flagship The future of long‑haul aviation is being shaped by two very different philosophies: Boeing’s high‑capacity 777-9 and Airbus’s ultra‑efficient A350‑1000. As we all know, Airbus holds a key advantage—the A350‑1000 has been flying for years, giving airlines the benefit of real‑world performance data, easier maintenance planning, and predictable operating costs. Boeing’s upcoming 777-9, set for entry into service around 2027, takes a bold approach: a wider fuselage, seating roughly 50–60 more passengers than the A350‑1000, and a massive carbon‑fiber wing with folding tips for improved aerodynamic efficiency. Boeing expects its GE9X‑powered design to match or surpass the A350’s fuel‑burn advantage once fully operational. The A350‑1000, meanwhile, delivers long‑range capability (up to ~13,500 km) thanks to a lightweight carbon‑fiber structure and efficient Trent XWB‑97 engines, offering airlines up to 13% lower fuel burn and 16% lower operating costs compared to other widebodies. ✅ Where the Boeing 777X Excels * Highest capacity in its class (approx. 426 seats in a two class layout, 50-60 more than the A350-1000). * Advanced folding‑tip carbon‑fiber wing for superior aerodynamic performance. * Designed for carriers with dense long‑haul demand seeking maximum revenue per flight. ✅ Where the Airbus A350‑1000 Excels * Already in service with years of proven reliability and mature operating data. * Lower fuel consumption and operating costs due to its lightweight composite design. * Ideal for ultra‑long‑haul routes with moderate passenger loads. Some great news to send us all into the weekend! Being Ayrshire born and bred, I'm really delighted to see this which will have a significant long-term impact across Ayrshire and beyond. Congratulations to all involved! A significant boost for Ayrshire and Scotland’s aerospace sector today as Ryanair announces a £40 million investment to expand its heavy maintenance operations at Prestwick Airport. The investment will see Ryanair scale its existing maintenance facility from six to ten bays, making it the company's largest such facility. It will also create 450 new highly skilled engineering and mechanic jobs, including 60 apprenticeships 👥 [Scottish Enterprise](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/scottish-enterprise?trk=public_post-text) has backed this expansion with an £11.6 million funding package alongside £1.52 million for enabling infrastructure. Commenting, Scottish Enterprise Chief Executive, Adrian Gillespie, said: “The expansion will build on Prestwick’s strong track record in aircraft maintenance, increase productivity and create new high-quality jobs. It’s exactly the type of project our mission approach is designed to support.” Teams across Scottish Enterprise, including global investments, workplace innovation, property and infrastructure, worked closely with Ryanair and Prestwick Aircraft Maintenance Ltd (PAML), alongside the Scottish and UK Government, South Ayrshire Council and Prestwick Airport to support this investment. Ryanair CEO, Eddie Wilson, added: "Their focussed approach in backing this project has been crucial in enabling us to grow Prestwick into a major heavy maintenance and training hub that will deliver skilled careers and economic benefits for many years to come.” Read the full story ➡️ [https://ow.ly/4Sz330sUtlQ](https://www.l

People looking for mortgages are facing a new headache (Image: Patamaporn Umnahanant via Getty Images) Fresh analysis has revealed customers of many major banks facing fresh challenges as world events have transformed the landscape since the beginning of March. A reduction to Bank of England Base Rate (BBR) appears increasingly improbable, with the turmoil in mortgage re-pricing resulting in a disappearing act of sub-4% fixed mortgages, according to [Moneyfactscompare.co.uk](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http%3A%2F%2FMoneyfactscompare%2Eco%2Euk&urlhash=OJpH&trk=public_post-text) analysis published on Tuesday morning. Summarising the current situation, Moneyfactscompare said the pool of lenders offering a sub-4% fixed rate deal had taken a “significant blow”. All of the biggest banks, namely Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds Bank, NatWest and Santander, have raised rates since the beginning of March. It added that prolonged market uncertainty “can lead to further rate increases or product withdrawals. Barclays, HSBC, NatWest, Nationwide and Santander no longer offer sub-4% fixed deals, which were available last week”. Throughout the market, it said, the last time the lowest two- and five-year fixed rates were priced above 4% was more than a year ago, in February 2025, based on first-of-month data. Year-on-year average mortgage rates across the two, five and 10-year fixed sectors have declined, but recent rises have pushed the average two and five-year rates above five per cent, it said. The Bank of England Base Rate was reduced to 3.75% in December 2025. Since then, the average standard variable rate (SVR) has dropped by 0.14%, from 7.27% to 7.13%. This is just for the big five! 🤩 “Analysts anticipate a higher supply of debt being raised by the Big Five hyperscaler companies this year as they race to build out their data center infrastructure, following Amazon's near-record bond sale last week of roughly $54 billion in investment-grade bonds. Hyperscalers, which operate vast data centers and other infrastructure to facilitate AI training and deployment, have been raising debt to finance data centers needed to fuel the boom in AI. "There continues to be an expectation of a lot of capital to be raised in this sector," said John Servidea, co-head of investment-grade debt capital markets at JPMorgan, which led the Amazon deal. "Whether it's the companies' publicly stated capex ⁠budgets, or whether it's various banks' estimates of the amount of hyperscaler issuance, if you look at all of those, a realistic expectation would be that at some point there's more," Servidea added. Analysts at BofA Global Research on Friday raised their forecast for the hyperscalers' new debt in 2026 to $175 billion from $140 billion. In early February, Barclays analysts said that U.S. investment-grade corporate bond issuance could be greater than $2 trillion in 2026, which they said "would exceed even the post‑COVID record …”
