There's a lot of discourse right now about taste and judgment increasing in value in an AI world. Most of it lands on craft and aesthetics. And that's fine. But these are subjective qualities and not useful for clear and structured decision-making. I think the most operational version of that is knowing which constraints in your workflow are synthetic overhead and which are load-bearing quality gates. Compress the former as hard as you can. Protect the latter. For [Metaforms](https://www.linkedin.com/company/metaformsai?trk=public_post-text), this looks like a team compressing scripting from a few days to a few hours and then figuring out where that saved time goes. Does that time come off the overall delivery timeline, or does it go somewhere else in the project? I think the answer depends on what kind of bottleneck you're looking at. Scripting, QA, tabulations, charting. These are processing steps. Someone writes code, runs checks, formats output. Given the right tooling, these can be compressed by quite a bit. A huge fraction of the time spent here is recoverable in principle. But then there's fielding. Respondents are real people with real attention spans and real lives, and what they give you correlates with how much time they had to give it. An LOI of 25 minutes is 25 minutes. A field period truncated to accommodate upstream delays reflects in data quality. I don't think you can AI your way around this part. The tech is structurally irrelevant here. So you can use the recovered time to move the whole project forward and give field more room to run. Or you can use it to compress end-to-end research delivery time. Or you can do both! You can both get faster delivery AND better data, because field time stops being the variable that gets squeezed. Usually, most workflows have so much overhead stacked on top that they're

🌏 Insurance AI: 900+ use cases — and still not scaling 🔍 More use cases do not mean more scale Allianz reports more than 900 AI use cases internally registered worldwide. Yet, speaking at InsurTech Insights, Jason Howes of Allianz UK noted that scaling AI remains difficult, as reported by Insurance Post (Mar 19, 2026). This is not a contradiction. “900 use cases” does not mean 900 scaled deployments. It reflects a portfolio of initiatives — from pilots to partial implementations — across the group. AI is already widespread. But most use cases remain local. They work within specific teams, on specific workflows, under specific assumptions. What they rarely do is scale across: systems workflows decision ownership That is where scaling becomes difficult. ⸻ ⚖️ Not a regulatory issue The EU, under the EU AI Act, pushes for structured, risk-based deployment. The UK, guided by institutions such as the Financial Conduct Authority, takes a more principles-based approach. Different models. Same pattern. Even in more flexible environments, AI does not automatically scale. Because the constraint is not regulatory permission. It is operational integration. ⸻ 🧩 What this means for insurers Scaling AI is not about launching more use cases. It requires: • standardising workflows where decisions can be made • defining clear ownership of AI-driven decisions • designing systems to integrate, not isolate, AI capabilities Without this, AI remains a collection of initiatives rather than an operating capability. ⸻ 🧭 Key message AI does not fail at scale because of individual firms. It fails because large organisations are not designed to absorb it consistently. [Snowflake](https://www.linkedin.com/company/snowflake-computing?trk=public_post-text)’s INFER_SCHEMA for schema discovery on semi-structured data. With a simple SQL function, you can automatically detect file schemas (Parquet, Avro, ORC, JSON, CSV) directly from staged files and use that output to create tables or external/Iceberg tables effortlessly, all inline with Snowflake’s SQL experience. In contrast, traditional schema inference with tools like AWS Glue Crawlers requires a separate service that scans data sources, infers schema, and writes metadata to a catalog. Crawlers read sample records, estimate schema similarity, and can generate multiple table definitions depending on data variations. The [Snowflake](https://www.linkedin.com/company/snowflake-computing?trk=public_post-text) approach keeps schema discovery closer to the data warehouse, simplifies workflows, and avoids managing an external crawling service, making it a more seamless choice for teams that want to reduce overhead and stay within the SQL ecosystem. PharmaBlock Sciences (Nanjing), Inc. (SZSE:300725) is a leading provider of innovative chemistry products & services throughout the pharmaceutical R&D process and commercial production. Its core businesses include: a rationally designed building blocks (BBs) collection, supplying from discovery, to development & commercial; development & manufacturing of RSMs, intermediates & APIs for drug development & commercial. 

Officially operated in 2008, PharmaBlock has rapidly gained recognition for its outstanding capabilities in the design, synthesis, & supply of novel BBs. It has generated a large and unique collection of high-quality and diverse BBs, which are increasingly adopted by drug hunters to enrich the structure options, improve compound quality, expand patent space and eventually accelerate drug discovery projects. Inspired by the BBs, it has established a powerful drug discovery platform for medicinal chemists to quickly identify hit/lead. The platform includes a set of efficient tools, including but not limited to FBDD, DELT, & Virtual Screening.

Following the new molecules advancing from discovery to development, and into the market, the company is committed to a more comprehensive & long-term service to valued customers. In addition to strengthening process R&D capabilities & mfg. capacity to secure the sustainable supply of BBs, the company is providing CDMO solutions of RSMs, key intermediates & APIs. To further realize safe, efficient and green process & mfg., it has developed an advanced chemistry & engineering technology platform, featuring flow chemistry, micropacked bed hydrogenation, catalysis, crystallization, solid state chemistry etc. 

Over the past years, PharmaBlock has partnered with almost all the top pharmaceutical companies, and hundreds of small to medium-sized biotechs around the world. Our ever changing mission is to support clients to accelerate drug discovery & development, and move the new molecule to commercial in full speed.
